image.title: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Rhodium(I)‐Catalyzed P(III)‐Directed C–H Alkylation of Biarylphosphines With Maleimides
item.dc:description:
A Rh(I)‐catalyzed P(III)‐directed ortho‐C–H bond alkylation of biarylphosphines with maleimides is disclosed. The reaction accommodates substituted alkenes, proceeds efficiently on gram scale, and consistently generates diastereomeric phosphines. NMR and mechanistic studies reveal that the stereochemical outcome arises from conformational restriction of the C–C biaryl axis upon incorporation of the maleimide fragment, offering new opportunities for ligand diversification.
A Rh(I)‐catalyzed P(III)‐directed ortho‐CH bond alkylation of biarylphosphines with maleimides is disclosed. The transformation extends phosphine‐directed CH bond alkylation to activated, substituted alkenes that introduce a stereogenic center during CC bond formation. A Rh(I)/acetate manifold enables efficient coupling of diverse N‐alkyl and N‐aryl maleimides with electronically varied biarylphosphines, including one exemple on gram scale. The products are obtained as mixtures of two diastereoisomers. Detailed 2D NMR studies, together with DFT calculations, show that the observed isomerism originates from the combination of the newly formed stereogenic center and restricted rotation about the biaryl C(aryl)–C(aryl) bond. Kinetic isotope effect and intermolecular competition experiments support a pathway involving phosphine‐directed CH bond activation, alkene insertion into a Rh–H intermediate, and reversible hydride transfer. Overall, maleimides emerge as competent partners for P(III)‐directed CH bond alkylation and provide access to amide‐functionalized phosphines with stereochemically rich conformational landscapes.
item.content:encoded:
A Rh(I)-catalyzed P(III)-directed ortho-C–H bond alkylation of biarylphosphines with maleimides is disclosed. The reaction accommodates substituted alkenes, proceeds efficiently on gram scale, and consistently generates diastereomeric phosphines. NMR and mechanistic studies reveal that the stereochemical outcome arises from conformational restriction of the C–C biaryl axis upon incorporation of the maleimide fragment, offering new opportunities for ligand diversification.


A Rh(I)-catalyzed P(III)-directed ortho-CH bond alkylation of biarylphosphines with maleimides is disclosed. The transformation extends phosphine-directed CH bond alkylation to activated, substituted alkenes that introduce a stereogenic center during CC bond formation. A Rh(I)/acetate manifold enables efficient coupling of diverse N-alkyl and N-aryl maleimides with electronically varied biarylphosphines, including one exemple on gram scale. The products are obtained as mixtures of two diastereoisomers. Detailed 2D NMR studies, together with DFT calculations, show that the observed isomerism originates from the combination of the newly formed stereogenic center and restricted rotation about the biaryl C(aryl)–C(aryl) bond. Kinetic isotope effect and intermolecular competition experiments support a pathway involving phosphine-directed CH bond activation, alkene insertion into a Rh–H intermediate, and reversible hydride transfer. Overall, maleimides emerge as competent partners for P(III)-directed CH bond alkylation and provide access to amide-functionalized phosphines with stereochemically rich conformational landscapes.
item.dc:creator: Jian Zhang, Tao Ma, Marie‐Noelle Rager, E. Daiann Sosa Carrizo, Jean‐François Soulé
item.dc:title: Rhodium(I)‐Catalyzed P(III)‐Directed C–H Alkylation of Biarylphosphines With Maleimides

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Stereoretentive Protection and Deprotection of Chiral Silanol
item.dc:description: In this study, we demonstrate that allylic groups serve as versatile protective groups for the hydroxy group of chiral silanols. The protection and the deprotection are accomplished through Williamson‐type etherification and the Tsuji–Trost‐type reaction, respectively, in a highly stereoretentive manner. Furthermore, we found that a benzylic ether can be used as an orthogonal protective group to allyl protection. Using them, we achieved enantiodivergent synthesis of a chiral alkoxysilanol from one enantiomer of chiral dialkoxysilane.
item.content:encoded: In this study, we demonstrate that allylic groups serve as versatile protective groups for the hydroxy group of chiral silanols. The protection and the deprotection are accomplished through Williamson-type etherification and the Tsuji–Trost-type reaction, respectively, in a highly stereoretentive manner. Furthermore, we found that a benzylic ether can be used as an orthogonal protective group to allyl protection. Using them, we achieved enantiodivergent synthesis of a chiral alkoxysilanol from one enantiomer of chiral dialkoxysilane.
item.dc:creator: Aoi Tsukimi, Sora Nozaki, Saya Yamaguchi, Mayaka Maeno, Yuuya Kawasaki, Kazunobu Igawa, Katsuhiko Tomooka
item.dc:title: Stereoretentive Protection and Deprotection of Chiral Silanol

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Selective Oxidation of Primary Amines to Nitriles Catalyzed by the Ru‐Doped Hexagonal Perovskite SrMnO3
item.dc:description: The selective oxidation of primary amines to generate nitriles provides a cost‐effective and atom‐economical synthetic approach that avoids the formation of harmful byproducts. However, achieving high yields under mild conditions remains challenging, due to the difficulty in efficiently activating the α‐CH bonds of amines and the multiple competing pathways that reduce selectivity. The present study employed Ru‐substituted hexagonal perovskite nanoparticles (SrMn1−xRuxO3) as a highly effective heterogeneous catalyst for the aerobic oxidation of benzylamine at 40°C in air and without additives, affording benzonitrile in quantitative yields. The selectivity of this reaction for nitriles or dimeric secondary imines could be switched simply by changing the solvent. The catalyst exhibited excellent reusability without any significant loss of catalytic performance and was found to be applicable to a broad range of aromatic and aliphatic primary amines, yielding the corresponding nitriles. Mechanistic studies suggest that this reaction proceeds through the Mars–van Krevelen mechanism, in which scission of the α‐CH bond of a strongly adsorbed amine is the rate‐determining step. The incorporation of Ru in the catalyst evidently enhanced the reactivity of surface metal‐oxygen species, thereby facilitating α‐CH bond scission and enabling efficient nitrile synthesis under remarkably mild conditions.
item.content:encoded: The selective oxidation of primary amines to generate nitriles provides a cost-effective and atom-economical synthetic approach that avoids the formation of harmful byproducts. However, achieving high yields under mild conditions remains challenging, due to the difficulty in efficiently activating the α-CH bonds of amines and the multiple competing pathways that reduce selectivity. The present study employed Ru-substituted hexagonal perovskite nanoparticles (SrMn1−xRuxO3) as a highly effective heterogeneous catalyst for the aerobic oxidation of benzylamine at 40°C in air and without additives, affording benzonitrile in quantitative yields. The selectivity of this reaction for nitriles or dimeric secondary imines could be switched simply by changing the solvent. The catalyst exhibited excellent reusability without any significant loss of catalytic performance and was found to be applicable to a broad range of aromatic and aliphatic primary amines, yielding the corresponding nitriles. Mechanistic studies suggest that this reaction proceeds through the Mars–van Krevelen mechanism, in which scission of the α-CH bond of a strongly adsorbed amine is the rate-determining step. The incorporation of Ru in the catalyst evidently enhanced the reactivity of surface metal-oxygen species, thereby facilitating α-CH bond scission and enabling efficient nitrile synthesis under remarkably mild conditions.
item.dc:creator: Tsukino Ishizaki, Keiju Wachi, Keigo Kamata
item.dc:title: Selective Oxidation of Primary Amines to Nitriles Catalyzed by the Ru‐Doped Hexagonal Perovskite SrMnO3

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Photoinduced Transformations Toward Oxindole and Isoquinolinedione Scaffolds via Orthogonal Halogen Bonding Interactions and Halogen Atom Transfer (XAT)
item.dc:description:
An efficient strategy for the photoinduced halogen‐bond‐mediated activation of inert alkyl halides, further their participation in radical addition and cascade cyclization reactions with N‐arylacrylamides and N‐acryloyl benzamides, was developed. Mild, catalyst‐free conditions, broad substrate scope, and suitability for late‐stage functionalization, including the synthesis of pharmaceutically relevant functionalized oxindoles and isoquinolinediones, are the advantageous of the protocol.
Herein, we report a strategy for the photoinduced halogen‐bond‐mediated activation of inert alkyl halides, enabling their participation in radical addition and cascade cyclization reactions with N‐arylacrylamides and N‐acryloyl benzamides. This approach affords a range of functionalized oxindoles and isoquinolinediones, including pharmaceutically relevant compounds, in good to excellent yields. Combined experimental and computational investigations reveal that halogen‐bonding interactions between amine/iodoarene and amine/alkyl halide pairs play a crucial role in facilitating the transformation. Upon light irradiation, the predominant pathway involves the generation of aryl radicals and α‐amino alkyl radicals, which subsequently activate the alkyl iodide through a halogen atom transfer (XAT) mechanism.
item.content:encoded:
An efficient strategy for the photoinduced halogen-bond-mediated activation of inert alkyl halides, further their participation in radical addition and cascade cyclization reactions with N-arylacrylamides and N-acryloyl benzamides, was developed. Mild, catalyst-free conditions, broad substrate scope, and suitability for late-stage functionalization, including the synthesis of pharmaceutically relevant functionalized oxindoles and isoquinolinediones, are the advantageous of the protocol.


Herein, we report a strategy for the photoinduced halogen-bond-mediated activation of inert alkyl halides, enabling their participation in radical addition and cascade cyclization reactions with N-arylacrylamides and N-acryloyl benzamides. This approach affords a range of functionalized oxindoles and isoquinolinediones, including pharmaceutically relevant compounds, in good to excellent yields. Combined experimental and computational investigations reveal that halogen-bonding interactions between amine/iodoarene and amine/alkyl halide pairs play a crucial role in facilitating the transformation. Upon light irradiation, the predominant pathway involves the generation of aryl radicals and α-amino alkyl radicals, which subsequently activate the alkyl iodide through a halogen atom transfer (XAT) mechanism.
item.dc:creator: Avishek Kumar Jha, Gautham S. Kana, Ananthakrishna Panuganti, Gayathri K. L., Veera Reddy Yatham
item.dc:title: Photoinduced Transformations Toward Oxindole and Isoquinolinedione Scaffolds via Orthogonal Halogen Bonding Interactions and Halogen Atom Transfer (XAT)

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Substrate‐Controlled Organocatalytic Divergent Annulations of γ, γ’‐Alkylidene Indane‐1,3‐Diones With Enals
item.dc:description:
A substrate‐dependent, regio‐ and stereocontrolled diversity‐oriented organocatalytic vinylogous Michael addition/cyclization cascade of γ, γ’‐alkylidene indane‐1,3‐diones with enals, using secondary amines as the catalyst, has been developed.
A substrate‐dependent, regio‐ and stereo‐controlled diversity‐oriented organocatalytic vinylogous Michael addition/cyclization cascade of γ, γ’‐alkylidene indane‐1,3‐diones with enals, using secondary amines as the catalyst has been developed. These present methodologies allow the synthesis of chiral 5‐aryl cyclohexenone derivatives 5, bicyclic [3.3.1] acetal‐fused indane‐1,3‐diones 6, and spiroindane‐1,3‐diones 7 in a highly selective manner. Plausible catalytic pathways for the formation of these indane‐1,3‐dione derivatives were proposed. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations have clarified and supported how the organocatalyst is able to activate the diethyl‐substituted indane‐1,3‐diones 3 and enals 2, and an unusual organocatalytic vinylogous Michael addition/dual‐oxa Diels–Alder mechanism occurred to obtain 6.
item.content:encoded:
A substrate-dependent, regio- and stereocontrolled diversity-oriented organocatalytic vinylogous Michael addition/cyclization cascade of γ, γ’-alkylidene indane-1,3-diones with enals, using secondary amines as the catalyst, has been developed.


A substrate-dependent, regio- and stereo-controlled diversity-oriented organocatalytic vinylogous Michael addition/cyclization cascade of γ, γ’-alkylidene indane-1,3-diones with enals, using secondary amines as the catalyst has been developed. These present methodologies allow the synthesis of chiral 5-aryl cyclohexenone derivatives 5, bicyclic [3.3.1] acetal-fused indane-1,3-diones 6, and spiroindane-1,3-diones 7 in a highly selective manner. Plausible catalytic pathways for the formation of these indane-1,3-dione derivatives were proposed. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations have clarified and supported how the organocatalyst is able to activate the diethyl-substituted indane-1,3-diones 3 and enals 2, and an unusual organocatalytic vinylogous Michael addition/dual-oxa Diels–Alder mechanism occurred to obtain 6.
item.dc:creator: Jen‐Yu Kuan, Bo‐Wei Huang, Jeng‐Liang Han
item.dc:title: Substrate‐Controlled Organocatalytic Divergent Annulations of γ, γ’‐Alkylidene Indane‐1,3‐Diones With Enals

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Enaminone Triggered Ring Opening of Cyclopropenones for Enamide Synthesis and Its Application in Oxazole Construction
item.dc:description:
The ring opening reactions of cyclopropenones with the nucleophilic addition for the N‐site of enaminones has been achieved with Ag(I) catalysis, leading to the practical synthesis of enamides with N‐enone structure. The application of these enamides in the synthesis of oxazoles has been realized via intramolecular annulation.
Herein, we report an addition‐elimination ring opening reaction cascade between cyclopropenones and enaminones with Ag(I) catalysis. The successful utilization of enaminones as the carbon nucleophiles enables the practical synthesis of enamides via ring opening. In addition, the two‐step operation by subjecting the in situ generated enamides to oxidative conditions allows the synthesis of oxazoles featuring a C2‐vinyl substituent by CO bond construction.
item.content:encoded:
The ring opening reactions of cyclopropenones with the nucleophilic addition for the N-site of enaminones has been achieved with Ag(I) catalysis, leading to the practical synthesis of enamides with N-enone structure. The application of these enamides in the synthesis of oxazoles has been realized via intramolecular annulation.


Herein, we report an addition-elimination ring opening reaction cascade between cyclopropenones and enaminones with Ag(I) catalysis. The successful utilization of enaminones as the carbon nucleophiles enables the practical synthesis of enamides via ring opening. In addition, the two-step operation by subjecting the in situ generated enamides to oxidative conditions allows the synthesis of oxazoles featuring a C2-vinyl substituent by CO bond construction.
item.dc:creator: Xinyuan Wen, Changfeng Wan, Jie‐Ping Wan, Yunyun Liu
item.dc:title: Enaminone Triggered Ring Opening of Cyclopropenones for Enamide Synthesis and Its Application in Oxazole Construction

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Highly Selective Generation of 1O2 via Peroxymonosulfate Activation by Nonmetal P‐Doped Carbon Materials
item.dc:description: Singlet oxygen (1O2) is an excellent active species in green organic synthesis and water purification. However, to achieve efficient and selective generation of 1O2, complicated preparation processes of the catalysts are always needed. Herein, we demonstrated a facile and scalable strategy to fabricate P‐doped nonmetal carbon catalysts for the selective generation of 1O2 via peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation. Experimental and theoretical calculation results verified that C2PO2/CPO3 were the active sites, which effectively activated PMS and promoted the generation of 1O2 with 100% selectivity. The excellent oxidation ability of 1O2 promoted exceptional reaction rate for the removal of organic pollutants and strong resistance to interference from environmental media. Meanwhile, the turnover number value of the oxidation of benzylic alcohol to benzaldehyde could reach 0.151 at room temperature with 100% selectivity, which is superior to most of the reported carbon catalysts. This work provides new strategy of simple yet effective catalysts for the selective production of 1O2, holding great promise for diverse applications including environmental remediation and organic synthesis.
item.content:encoded: Singlet oxygen (1O2) is an excellent active species in green organic synthesis and water purification. However, to achieve efficient and selective generation of 1O2, complicated preparation processes of the catalysts are always needed. Herein, we demonstrated a facile and scalable strategy to fabricate P-doped nonmetal carbon catalysts for the selective generation of 1O2 via peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation. Experimental and theoretical calculation results verified that C2PO2/CPO3 were the active sites, which effectively activated PMS and promoted the generation of 1O2 with 100% selectivity. The excellent oxidation ability of 1O2 promoted exceptional reaction rate for the removal of organic pollutants and strong resistance to interference from environmental media. Meanwhile, the turnover number value of the oxidation of benzylic alcohol to benzaldehyde could reach 0.151 at room temperature with 100% selectivity, which is superior to most of the reported carbon catalysts. This work provides new strategy of simple yet effective catalysts for the selective production of 1O2, holding great promise for diverse applications including environmental remediation and organic synthesis.
item.dc:creator: Yu Hou, Yuan Tao, Shunbing Su, Xi Chen, Qi Fu, Huajie Zhong, Jiaxing Yu, Zeyu Gong, Ludan Yu, Shanshan Wu, Lezhou Yi, Junhui Wang, Fang Zhu, Gangfeng Ouyang
item.dc:title: Highly Selective Generation of 1O2 via Peroxymonosulfate Activation by Nonmetal P‐Doped Carbon Materials

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: A Highly Efficient Enzymatic Method for the Sustainable and Stereoselective Production of 5α‐H Key Intermediates for Steroidal Pharmaceuticals
item.dc:description:
A 5α‐reductase (5αRed‐9) from a Prevotellaceae bacterium enables highly diastereoselective reduction of androstenedione (>99% de) and exhibits broad substrate tolerance toward diverse steroids. In preparative reactions, the product was obtained in 99% purity, providing a practical and selective route to key 5α‐hydrogenated intermediates for steroidal pharmaceuticals.
A 5α‐reductase (5αRed‐9) from a Prevotellaceae bacterium was identified to catalyze the quantitative reduction of androstenedione with >99% de and exhibit a broad substrate scope. In the preparative‐scale reaction, 5α‐AD was isolated in 90% yield and 99% purity, with 13.3 gL−1 d−1 space‐time yield. This biocatalytic process overcomes the selectivity limitations of chemical methods, offering an efficient and sustainable strategy for industrial‐scale production.
item.content:encoded:
A 5α-reductase (5αRed-9) from a Prevotellaceae bacterium enables highly diastereoselective reduction of androstenedione (>99% de) and exhibits broad substrate tolerance toward diverse steroids. In preparative reactions, the product was obtained in 99% purity, providing a practical and selective route to key 5α-hydrogenated intermediates for steroidal pharmaceuticals.


A 5α-reductase (5αRed-9) from a Prevotellaceae bacterium was identified to catalyze the quantitative reduction of androstenedione with >99% de and exhibit a broad substrate scope. In the preparative-scale reaction, 5α-AD was isolated in 90% yield and 99% purity, with 13.3 gL−1 d−1 space-time yield. This biocatalytic process overcomes the selectivity limitations of chemical methods, offering an efficient and sustainable strategy for industrial-scale production.
item.dc:creator: Chi Lu, Yu Wang, Rui Zhang, Xi Chen, Zhengbin Zhang, Xuejie Qi, Jinhui Feng, Qiaqing Wu, Dunming Zhu
item.dc:title: A Highly Efficient Enzymatic Method for the Sustainable and Stereoselective Production of 5α‐H Key Intermediates for Steroidal Pharmaceuticals

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Recent Advances in the Synthesis of Nitrogen‐Containing Heterocycles from o‐Aminobenzamides: Reactions and Mechanism
item.dc:description:
This review systematically consolidates and analyzes the significant progress over the past decade (2015–2025) in constructing privileged nitrogen‐containing heterocycles, specifically quinazolinones, quinazoline‐2,4‐diones, benzodiazepinediones, acridones, and benzotriazinones using o‐aminobenzamides as a versatile platform molecule.
Nitrogen‐containing heterocycles constitute a pivotal class of core structural scaffolds, notable for their significant biological activities and functional versatility. Consequently, the development of efficient, green, and atom‐economical methodologies for the construction of N‐heterocycles represents a central pursuit in modern synthetic chemistry. o‐Aminobenzamides have emerged as versatile platform molecules in this context. Their molecular architecture, featuring a nucleophilic amino group, an electrophilic amide carbonyl, and a readily modifiable aromatic ring, enables efficient construction of CN and NN bonds via condensation and cyclization reactions, rendering them key starting materials for accessing diverse N‐heterocycles. This review systematically summarizes research advances from the past decade (2015–2025) on the synthesis of N‐heterocyclic systems including quinazolinones, quinazoline diones, benzodiazepinediones, acridones, and benzotriazinones from o‐aminobenzamides with various “C1 sources” (e.g., aldehydes, alcohols, ketones, carboxylic acids, esters), “N1 sources” (e.g., nitromethane, tert‐butyl nitrite, nitrates), and other functional reagents. The discussion focuses on the design strategies of reaction pathways and the mechanisms underpinning key cyclization steps, such as intramolecular nucleophilic attack, oxidative cyclization, and radical processes. A comparative analysis is provided on the advantages and limitations of different catalytic systems, including transition‐metal catalysis, catalyst‐free conditions, photocatalysis, electrochemical synthesis, and heterogeneous catalysis, with respect to reaction efficiency, regioselectivity, and functional group tolerance. These methodological advances not only expand the synthetic toolkit for N‐heterocycle construction but also offer robust support for the rapid discovery and structural optimization of drug leads.
item.content:encoded:
This review systematically consolidates and analyzes the significant progress over the past decade (2015–2025) in constructing privileged nitrogen-containing heterocycles, specifically quinazolinones, quinazoline-2,4-diones, benzodiazepinediones, acridones, and benzotriazinones using o-aminobenzamides as a versatile platform molecule.


Nitrogen-containing heterocycles constitute a pivotal class of core structural scaffolds, notable for their significant biological activities and functional versatility. Consequently, the development of efficient, green, and atom-economical methodologies for the construction of N-heterocycles represents a central pursuit in modern synthetic chemistry. o-Aminobenzamides have emerged as versatile platform molecules in this context. Their molecular architecture, featuring a nucleophilic amino group, an electrophilic amide carbonyl, and a readily modifiable aromatic ring, enables efficient construction of CN and NN bonds via condensation and cyclization reactions, rendering them key starting materials for accessing diverse N-heterocycles. This review systematically summarizes research advances from the past decade (2015–2025) on the synthesis of N-heterocyclic systems including quinazolinones, quinazoline diones, benzodiazepinediones, acridones, and benzotriazinones from o-aminobenzamides with various “C1 sources” (e.g., aldehydes, alcohols, ketones, carboxylic acids, esters), “N1 sources” (e.g., nitromethane, tert-butyl nitrite, nitrates), and other functional reagents. The discussion focuses on the design strategies of reaction pathways and the mechanisms underpinning key cyclization steps, such as intramolecular nucleophilic attack, oxidative cyclization, and radical processes. A comparative analysis is provided on the advantages and limitations of different catalytic systems, including transition-metal catalysis, catalyst-free conditions, photocatalysis, electrochemical synthesis, and heterogeneous catalysis, with respect to reaction efficiency, regioselectivity, and functional group tolerance. These methodological advances not only expand the synthetic toolkit for N-heterocycle construction but also offer robust support for the rapid discovery and structural optimization of drug leads.
item.dc:creator: Xiaofeng Hua, Nina Cheng, Shiwei Zhu, Yatang Wang, Yingying Wu, Wei Guo, Lvyin Zheng
item.dc:title: Recent Advances in the Synthesis of Nitrogen‐Containing Heterocycles from o‐Aminobenzamides: Reactions and Mechanism

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Recent Advances in Photocatalytic Cleavage Reactions of Halogenated Difluoromethyl Reagents
item.dc:description:
Photocatalytic radical cleavage of halodifluoromethyl reagents generates a variety of synthetic building blocks for organic synthesis. These intermediates have been widely applied in the construction of diverse molecular frameworks and functional group transformations. Future developments in this field are expected to promote more sustainable and precise multidomain reactions.
Halogenated difluoromethyl reagents serve as carbon synthons, finding applications in the construction of diverse molecular scaffolds and functional group modification reactions. Consequently, their conversion reactions and applications have attracted considerable attention. Recently, within the halogenated reagent cleavage paradigm dominated by electron transfer, its single‐cleavage, double‐cleavage, and multicleavage modes have yielded diverse synthetic building blocks. These have been applied in various radical reactions, facilitating the synthesis of numerous high‐value compounds and promoting the development of a series of highly efficient green synthetic methodologies. In this context, we review recent advances in photocatalysis‐dominated cleavage reactions of halogenated reagents, highlighting the recent achievements in this field.
item.content:encoded:
Photocatalytic radical cleavage of halodifluoromethyl reagents generates a variety of synthetic building blocks for organic synthesis. These intermediates have been widely applied in the construction of diverse molecular frameworks and functional group transformations. Future developments in this field are expected to promote more sustainable and precise multidomain reactions.


Halogenated difluoromethyl reagents serve as carbon synthons, finding applications in the construction of diverse molecular scaffolds and functional group modification reactions. Consequently, their conversion reactions and applications have attracted considerable attention. Recently, within the halogenated reagent cleavage paradigm dominated by electron transfer, its single-cleavage, double-cleavage, and multicleavage modes have yielded diverse synthetic building blocks. These have been applied in various radical reactions, facilitating the synthesis of numerous high-value compounds and promoting the development of a series of highly efficient green synthetic methodologies. In this context, we review recent advances in photocatalysis-dominated cleavage reactions of halogenated reagents, highlighting the recent achievements in this field.
item.dc:creator: Biao Yao, Lin Wang, Tianyu Wang, Yu Zhang, Haoran Zhen, Lujun Zhang, Lei Wang
item.dc:title: Recent Advances in Photocatalytic Cleavage Reactions of Halogenated Difluoromethyl Reagents

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Regio‐ and Stereo‐Selective CH Linear Dienylation of Diverse Arenes and Heteroarenes
item.dc:description:
Rh(III)‐catalyzed C‐H dienylation of a diverse array of arenes and heteroarenes has been demonstrated with linear selectivity engaging allenyl carbinol acetates or 1,3‐dienes as dienylating agents.
Herein, we report two distinct Rh(III)‐catalyzed CH activation strategies for the stereoselective synthesis of linear 1,3‐dienes employing allenyl carbinol acetates or 1,3‐dienes as [4C]‐dienylating agents. Both protocols accommodate a broad range of aryl and heteroaryl substrates, including indole, indoline, thiophene, pyrrole, benzothiophene, and dibenzofuran. The versatility and robustness of these protocols are further demonstrated through gram‐scale synthesis and postsynthetic functionalizations of the resulting dienes, underscoring their synthetic utility. Moreover, photophysical studies of the products indicate their potential applicability for optoelectronic applications. Rh(III)‐catalyzed C‐H dienylation of a diverse array of arenes and heteroarenes has been demonstrated with linear selectivity engaging allenyl carbinol acetates or 1,3‐dienes as dienylating agents.
item.content:encoded:
Rh(III)-catalyzed C-H dienylation of a diverse array of arenes and heteroarenes has been demonstrated with linear selectivity engaging allenyl carbinol acetates or 1,3-dienes as dienylating agents.


Herein, we report two distinct Rh(III)-catalyzed CH activation strategies for the stereoselective synthesis of linear 1,3-dienes employing allenyl carbinol acetates or 1,3-dienes as [4C]-dienylating agents. Both protocols accommodate a broad range of aryl and heteroaryl substrates, including indole, indoline, thiophene, pyrrole, benzothiophene, and dibenzofuran. The versatility and robustness of these protocols are further demonstrated through gram-scale synthesis and postsynthetic functionalizations of the resulting dienes, underscoring their synthetic utility. Moreover, photophysical studies of the products indicate their potential applicability for optoelectronic applications. Rh(III)-catalyzed C-H dienylation of a diverse array of arenes and heteroarenes has been demonstrated with linear selectivity engaging allenyl carbinol acetates or 1,3-dienes as dienylating agents.
item.dc:creator: Vinod Deshmukh, Om Prakash Dash, Shivam Pandey, Chandra M. R. Volla
item.dc:title: Regio‐ and Stereo‐Selective CH Linear Dienylation of Diverse Arenes and Heteroarenes

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesis of a Full Spectrum of Indole‐Fused Medium‐Sized Lactams via Base‐Enabled Higher‐Order Annulation
item.dc:description:
A base‐enabled efficient synthesis of indole‐fused medium‐sized lactams has been established by designing 2‐indolylcarboxylic esters as tunable four‐carbon (4C) to seven‐carbon (7C) synthons for higher‐order annulations, providing an efficient access to a full spectrum of indole‐fused eight‐ to eleven‐membered medium‐sized lactams.
An efficient synthesis of indole‐fused medium‐sized lactams via higher‐order annulation is reported. This strategy employs 2‐indolylcarboxylate esters as flexible 4C to 7C synthons, which undergo higher‐order annulations with aza‐ortho‐quinone methides generated in situ from ortho‐chloromethyl anilines. The method enables access to a full spectrum of indole‐fused eight‐ to eleven‐membered lactams under mild conditions, featuring broad substrate scope and excellent functional group tolerance. This work not only establishes an efficient approach for constructing indole‐fused medium‐sized rings, but also significantly enriches the chemistry of indole‐fused heterocycles.
item.content:encoded:
A base-enabled efficient synthesis of indole-fused medium-sized lactams has been established by designing 2-indolylcarboxylic esters as tunable four-carbon (4C) to seven-carbon (7C) synthons for higher-order annulations, providing an efficient access to a full spectrum of indole-fused eight- to eleven-membered medium-sized lactams.


An efficient synthesis of indole-fused medium-sized lactams via higher-order annulation is reported. This strategy employs 2-indolylcarboxylate esters as flexible 4C to 7C synthons, which undergo higher-order annulations with aza-ortho-quinone methides generated in situ from ortho-chloromethyl anilines. The method enables access to a full spectrum of indole-fused eight- to eleven-membered lactams under mild conditions, featuring broad substrate scope and excellent functional group tolerance. This work not only establishes an efficient approach for constructing indole-fused medium-sized rings, but also significantly enriches the chemistry of indole-fused heterocycles.
item.dc:creator: Rui Li, Can Yang, Yi‐Fan Wang, Hao‐Hui Zhang, Kuiyong Dong, Feng Shi
item.dc:title: Synthesis of a Full Spectrum of Indole‐Fused Medium‐Sized Lactams via Base‐Enabled Higher‐Order Annulation

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Palladium‐Catalyzed Crosscoupling Stereoselective Construction of Dienes via Z‐Alkenylation of Vinyl Bromides
item.dc:description:
The added graphical abstract content is: A palladium‐catalyzed cross‐coupling between Z‐tertiary allylic alcohols with vinyl bromides via β‐carbon elimination has been developed. This procedure offering a valuable methodology for the construction of multisubstituted (Z,E)‐dienes. Late‐stage Z‐alkenylation of steroids has also been demonstrated.
Transition‐metal‐catalyzed stereoselective synthesis of 1,3‐dienes has witnessed considerable progress in recent years, but significant limitations remain. In this study, we report a palladium‐catalyzed crosscoupling between Z‐tertiary allylic alcohols and vinyl bromides via β‐carbon elimination. This reaction proceeds under mild conditions and exhibits broad substrate scopes and excellent Z/E stereoselectivity, offering a valuable methodology for the construction of multisubstituted (Z,E)‐dienes. Notably, this transformation enables efficient late‐stage Z‐alkenylation of steroid derivatives. Moreover, the resulting diene undergoes a Diels–Alder reaction with maleic anhydride to afford the 1,4‐cis‐endo monocyclic product.
item.content:encoded:
The added graphical abstract content is: A palladium-catalyzed cross-coupling between Z-tertiary allylic alcohols with vinyl bromides via β-carbon elimination has been developed. This procedure offering a valuable methodology for the construction of multisubstituted (Z,E)-dienes. Late-stage Z-alkenylation of steroids has also been demonstrated.


Transition-metal-catalyzed stereoselective synthesis of 1,3-dienes has witnessed considerable progress in recent years, but significant limitations remain. In this study, we report a palladium-catalyzed crosscoupling between Z-tertiary allylic alcohols and vinyl bromides via β-carbon elimination. This reaction proceeds under mild conditions and exhibits broad substrate scopes and excellent Z/E stereoselectivity, offering a valuable methodology for the construction of multisubstituted (Z,E)-dienes. Notably, this transformation enables efficient late-stage Z-alkenylation of steroid derivatives. Moreover, the resulting diene undergoes a Diels–Alder reaction with maleic anhydride to afford the 1,4-cis-endo monocyclic product.

item.dc:title: Palladium‐Catalyzed Crosscoupling Stereoselective Construction of Dienes via Z‐Alkenylation of Vinyl Bromides

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: TEMPO Oxidation of Phenylalkynes: A Green Approach to Diketophenols
item.dc:description:
Phenolic alkynes undergo an unusual 2,2,6,6‐tetramethylpiperidin‐1‐oxyl‐catalyzed aerobic oxidation to deliver 4‐hydroxybenzil derivatives under mild, metal‐free conditions. The transformation proceeds through oxidation of the phenolic alkyne to a reactive quinoethylene species, followed by oxygen‐ and water‐assisted conversion to diketones. This strategy enables efficient access to valuable benzil frameworks using molecular oxygen as a sustainable oxidant.
Herein, we report a catalytic 2,2,6,6‐tetramethylpiperidin‐1‐oxyl (TEMPO)‐mediated oxidation of 4‐hydroxyphenylalkynes under molecular oxygen, affording 4‐hydroxybenzil efficiently. Mechanistic studies suggest that TEMPO oxidizes the phenolic alkyne to a quinoethylene intermediate, which is further transformed into diketones in the presence of oxygen and/or water. This metal‐free protocol avoids stoichiometric strong oxidants, providing a green and operationally simple route to valuable diketone compounds.
item.content:encoded:
Phenolic alkynes undergo an unusual 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidin-1-oxyl-catalyzed aerobic oxidation to deliver 4-hydroxybenzil derivatives under mild, metal-free conditions. The transformation proceeds through oxidation of the phenolic alkyne to a reactive quinoethylene species, followed by oxygen- and water-assisted conversion to diketones. This strategy enables efficient access to valuable benzil frameworks using molecular oxygen as a sustainable oxidant.


Herein, we report a catalytic 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidin-1-oxyl (TEMPO)-mediated oxidation of 4-hydroxyphenylalkynes under molecular oxygen, affording 4-hydroxybenzil efficiently. Mechanistic studies suggest that TEMPO oxidizes the phenolic alkyne to a quinoethylene intermediate, which is further transformed into diketones in the presence of oxygen and/or water. This metal-free protocol avoids stoichiometric strong oxidants, providing a green and operationally simple route to valuable diketone compounds.
item.dc:creator: Zhiwen Zhong, Genshang Liu, Zhihan Sheng, Chenlong Zhu, Bingfeng Sun
item.dc:title: TEMPO Oxidation of Phenylalkynes: A Green Approach to Diketophenols

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Selective Electrochemical Hydrocarboxylation of Vinyl Phosphonates with CO2 Enabled by Alternating Polarity
item.dc:description:
Selective electrochemical hydrocarboxylation of vinyl phosphonates with CO2 is achieved via alternating polarity electrolysis, significantly enhancing Faraday efficiency and delivering high yields for a broad range of substrates.
Electrochemical hydrocarboxylation of vinyl phosphonates with CO2 to form carboxylic‐substituted phosphonates is developed. The alternating polarity affords a boost in Faraday efficiency and enables generalizable catalytic control to provide high product yields over a broad substrate scope by maintaining electrode activity through the regeneration of its surface. The disclosed method provides high selectivity of the target hydrocarboxylation without side transformations of vinyl phosphonates in an undivided cell affording a short way to valuable carboxylic acids featuring a phosphonate fragment. The high selectivity of hydrocarboxylation is achieved through the integration of an alternating polarity strategy with an efficient sequence of one‐electron reduction, followed by CC bond formation, subsequent second one‐electron reduction, and protonation. These results establish the utility of the alternating polarity technique and provide a foundation for its adaptation to other electrochemical reactions.
item.content:encoded:
Selective electrochemical hydrocarboxylation of vinyl phosphonates with CO2 is achieved via alternating polarity electrolysis, significantly enhancing Faraday efficiency and delivering high yields for a broad range of substrates.


Electrochemical hydrocarboxylation of vinyl phosphonates with CO2 to form carboxylic-substituted phosphonates is developed. The alternating polarity affords a boost in Faraday efficiency and enables generalizable catalytic control to provide high product yields over a broad substrate scope by maintaining electrode activity through the regeneration of its surface. The disclosed method provides high selectivity of the target hydrocarboxylation without side transformations of vinyl phosphonates in an undivided cell affording a short way to valuable carboxylic acids featuring a phosphonate fragment. The high selectivity of hydrocarboxylation is achieved through the integration of an alternating polarity strategy with an efficient sequence of one-electron reduction, followed by CC bond formation, subsequent second one-electron reduction, and protonation. These results establish the utility of the alternating polarity technique and provide a foundation for its adaptation to other electrochemical reactions.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Visible Light‐Induced Vinyl Radical Generation/Isomerization/Radical Coupling of Tetrasubstituted Vinyl Chlorides and Thiols Via Electron Donor–Acceptor Complexes
item.dc:description: A visible‐light‐driven, metal‐free reaction between γ‐lactam‐based 1,1‐chloro‐trifluoromethyl‐2‐alkenes and thiols has been developed, which proceeds via an electron donor−acceptor (EDA) complex‐enabled radical mechanism. The protocol allows for the rapid synthesis of diverse trifluoromethylated thioethers through a sequential process involving vinyl radical generation, double bond isomerization, and radical coupling under mild and operationally simple reaction conditions. Furthermore, preliminary biological evaluation revealed that several of the synthesized products display inhibitory activity against the MDA‐MB‐123 breast cell line.
item.content:encoded: A visible-light-driven, metal-free reaction between γ-lactam-based 1,1-chloro-trifluoromethyl-2-alkenes and thiols has been developed, which proceeds via an electron donor−acceptor (EDA) complex-enabled radical mechanism. The protocol allows for the rapid synthesis of diverse trifluoromethylated thioethers through a sequential process involving vinyl radical generation, double bond isomerization, and radical coupling under mild and operationally simple reaction conditions. Furthermore, preliminary biological evaluation revealed that several of the synthesized products display inhibitory activity against the MDA-MB-123 breast cell line.

item.dc:title: Visible Light‐Induced Vinyl Radical Generation/Isomerization/Radical Coupling of Tetrasubstituted Vinyl Chlorides and Thiols Via Electron Donor–Acceptor Complexes

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Mechanically Assisted PO, PN, and PS Bonds Formation
item.dc:description:
We present a general mechanochemical strategy for constructing PO, PN, and PS bonds via solvent‐free reactions of phosphoryl chlorides with O‐, N‐, and S‐nucleophiles. The method operates under mild conditions, shows broad substrate scope, and affords phosphates, phosphoramidates, and thiophosphates efficiently, providing a sustainable platform for organophosphorus synthesis.
We report a general and efficient mechanochemical protocol for the formation of PO, PN, and PS bonds through the reaction of phosphoryl chlorides with O‐, N‐, and S‐centered nucleophiles. The transformation proceeds under solvent‐free conditions using ball milling in the presence of triethylamine and an inert grinding auxiliary, under ambient atmosphere and short reaction times. A broad range of primary and secondary alcohols, phenols, amines, anilines, and thiols are compatible with the methodology, affording the corresponding phosphates, phosphoramidates, and thiophosphates in moderate‐to‐excellent yields.
item.content:encoded:
We present a general mechanochemical strategy for constructing PO, PN, and PS bonds via solvent-free reactions of phosphoryl chlorides with O-, N-, and S-nucleophiles. The method operates under mild conditions, shows broad substrate scope, and affords phosphates, phosphoramidates, and thiophosphates efficiently, providing a sustainable platform for organophosphorus synthesis.


We report a general and efficient mechanochemical protocol for the formation of PO, PN, and PS bonds through the reaction of phosphoryl chlorides with O-, N-, and S-centered nucleophiles. The transformation proceeds under solvent-free conditions using ball milling in the presence of triethylamine and an inert grinding auxiliary, under ambient atmosphere and short reaction times. A broad range of primary and secondary alcohols, phenols, amines, anilines, and thiols are compatible with the methodology, affording the corresponding phosphates, phosphoramidates, and thiophosphates in moderate-to-excellent yields.

item.dc:title: Mechanically Assisted PO, PN, and PS Bonds Formation

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Deuteration of Arenes in Pharmaceuticals by SEAr Mechanism with Heavy Water Using pTSA Catalyst
item.dc:description:
We present an hydrogen/deuterium exchange reaction using mild Brønsted acid para‐toluene sulfonic acid. We also demonstrated a wide range of applications across diverse bioactive molecules, pharmaceuticals, and small compounds, with as many as 54 examples. Efficiency of the reaction can be achieved on a gram‐scale by utilizing only 5.0 equivalents of D2O and a reduced catalyst loading of just 4.0 mol%.
Aromatic rings are widely recognized in the realm of natural products and bioactive molecules, highlighting their importance in various applications. The development of deuterated analogues of pharmaceuticals is particularly significant in pharmaceutical and medicinal chemistry. In this study, we present a novel catalytic method for hydrogen/deuterium (H/D) exchange in aromatic compounds, using cost‐effective, readily available D2O as the deuterium source. This approach demonstrates impressive deuterium incorporation under mild reaction conditions, yielding good to excellent results in a single run. Utilizing para‐toluenesulfonic acid as a catalyst, this metal‐free protocol exhibits broad applicability in the late‐stage deuteration of a variety of bioactive molecules, pharmaceuticals, and small molecules. Furthermore, our method is scalable; it has been successfully implemented at the gram‐scale with a reduced catalyst loading of 2.0 mol% for Tyrosol, illustrating its practical utility. Moreover, our thorough mechanistic studies and detailed density functional theory studies enhance our understanding of how the catalyst and solvent contribute to the aromatic electrophilic substitution process. This innovative method provides a sustainable alternative to traditional transition metal‐catalyzed H/D exchange techniques.
item.content:encoded:
We present an hydrogen/deuterium exchange reaction using mild Brønsted acid para-toluene sulfonic acid. We also demonstrated a wide range of applications across diverse bioactive molecules, pharmaceuticals, and small compounds, with as many as 54 examples. Efficiency of the reaction can be achieved on a gram-scale by utilizing only 5.0 equivalents of D2O and a reduced catalyst loading of just 4.0 mol%.


Aromatic rings are widely recognized in the realm of natural products and bioactive molecules, highlighting their importance in various applications. The development of deuterated analogues of pharmaceuticals is particularly significant in pharmaceutical and medicinal chemistry. In this study, we present a novel catalytic method for hydrogen/deuterium (H/D) exchange in aromatic compounds, using cost-effective, readily available D2O as the deuterium source. This approach demonstrates impressive deuterium incorporation under mild reaction conditions, yielding good to excellent results in a single run. Utilizing para-toluenesulfonic acid as a catalyst, this metal-free protocol exhibits broad applicability in the late-stage deuteration of a variety of bioactive molecules, pharmaceuticals, and small molecules. Furthermore, our method is scalable; it has been successfully implemented at the gram-scale with a reduced catalyst loading of 2.0 mol% for Tyrosol, illustrating its practical utility. Moreover, our thorough mechanistic studies and detailed density functional theory studies enhance our understanding of how the catalyst and solvent contribute to the aromatic electrophilic substitution process. This innovative method provides a sustainable alternative to traditional transition metal-catalyzed H/D exchange techniques.

item.dc:title: Deuteration of Arenes in Pharmaceuticals by SEAr Mechanism with Heavy Water Using pTSA Catalyst

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Visible‐Light‐Catalyzed CSi Bond Cleavage: Reaction Strategies and Research Advances
item.dc:description: Over the past decade, visible‐light photoredox catalysis has emerged as a powerful and sustainable platform for generating radical intermediates, enabling novel bond‐forming strategies under mild conditions. Organosilanes, due to their stability, accessibility, and tunable reactivity, have become versatile radical precursors through photoinduced oxidative CSi bond cleavage. This review provides the first systematic summary of recent advances in visible‐light‐promoted CSi bond cleavage, covering not only established photoredox and metal/photoredox synergistic catalysis but also emerging strategies such as photoinduced small‐molecule catalysis and photoinduced metal complex catalytic systems. This review also highlights two challenges that are currently under‐researched: the cleavage of nonactivated C(sp3)Si bonds and the selective CH functionalization of silanes. By focusing on catalyst design, this review analyzes how photosensitizer structure, metal–ligand environments, and chiral control units govern reaction efficiency and selectivity. Mechanistic insights and future directions are discussed, with the aim of inspiring the development of novel catalytic systems for sustainable synthesis and precision molecular editing.
item.content:encoded: Over the past decade, visible-light photoredox catalysis has emerged as a powerful and sustainable platform for generating radical intermediates, enabling novel bond-forming strategies under mild conditions. Organosilanes, due to their stability, accessibility, and tunable reactivity, have become versatile radical precursors through photoinduced oxidative CSi bond cleavage. This review provides the first systematic summary of recent advances in visible-light-promoted CSi bond cleavage, covering not only established photoredox and metal/photoredox synergistic catalysis but also emerging strategies such as photoinduced small-molecule catalysis and photoinduced metal complex catalytic systems. This review also highlights two challenges that are currently under-researched: the cleavage of nonactivated C(sp 3)Si bonds and the selective CH functionalization of silanes. By focusing on catalyst design, this review analyzes how photosensitizer structure, metal–ligand environments, and chiral control units govern reaction efficiency and selectivity. Mechanistic insights and future directions are discussed, with the aim of inspiring the development of novel catalytic systems for sustainable synthesis and precision molecular editing.

item.dc:title: Visible‐Light‐Catalyzed CSi Bond Cleavage: Reaction Strategies and Research Advances

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: The Electrochemical Frontier in Asymmetric Synthesis: Forging Challenging Quaternary Stereocenters
item.dc:description: Quaternary carbon stereocenters can be widely found in natural products, chiral pharmaceuticals, and functional materials, which stereocontrolled construction remains one of the most formidable challenges in asymmetric catalysis. Organic electrosynthesis, which uses electrons as traceless redox reagents, enables the precise generation and controlled conversion of highly reactive intermediates under mild conditions, thereby providing a green and efficient platform for the assembly of these congested stereocenters. This review highlights recent advances in the electrochemical asymmetric construction of quaternary stereocenters and offers a systematic overview of this rapidly emerging area. Organized according to substrate classes and reaction design principles, with mechanism/activation mode introduced as a secondary classification criterion, the discussion focuses on four major strategies based on sp2‐hybridized prochiral substrates: functionalization of poly‐substituted alkenes, transformation of prochiral enolates, nucleophilic addition to carbonyl compounds, and nucleophilic addition to imine derivatives. In addition, two distinctive approaches, namely indole dearomatization and electrochemical kinetic resolution, are summarized with emphasis on reaction mechanisms, modes of chiral induction, and substrate scope. The review further analyzes the key challenges that continue to limit this field, including catalyst development, expansion of reaction manifolds, and practical translation to large‐scale synthesis. Finally, future directions and opportunities are discussed with the aim of promoting the broader application of asymmetric electrochemical synthesis in the preparation of complex chiral molecules and the development of chiral pharmaceuticals.

Organic electrosynthesis, which uses electrons as traceless redox reagents, enables the precise generation and controlled conversion of highly reactive intermediates under mild conditions, thereby providing a green and efficient platform for the assembly of these congested stereocenters. This review highlights recent advances in the electrochemical asymmetric construction of quaternary stereocenters and offers a systematic overview of this rapidly emerging area. Organized according to substrate classes and reaction design principles, with mechanism/activation mode introduced as a secondary classification criterion, the discussion focuses on four major strategies based on sp2-hybridized prochiral substrates: functionalization of poly-substituted alkenes, transformation of prochiral enolates, nucleophilic addition to carbonyl compounds, and nucleophilic addition to imine derivatives. In addition, two distinctive approaches, namely indole dearomatization and electrochemical kinetic resolution, are summarized with emphasis on reaction mechanisms, modes of chiral induction, and substrate scope. The review further analyzes the key challenges that continue to limit this field, including catalyst development, expansion of reaction manifolds, and practical translation to large-scale synthesis. Finally, future directions and opportunities are discussed with the aim of promoting the broader application of asymmetric electrochemical synthesis in the preparation of complex chiral molecules and the development of chiral pharmaceuticals.

item.dc:title: The Electrochemical Frontier in Asymmetric Synthesis: Forging Challenging Quaternary Stereocenters

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Phosphine‐Catalyzed Double Aza‐Addition of Cyclohexadienone‐Tethered Alkynoates
item.dc:description:
A PPh3‐catalyzed sequential α‐umpolung addition/Michael addition of cyclohexadienone‐tethered alkynoates with sulfonamides constructs densely functionalized cis‐hydrobenzomorpholines. Sulfonamides serve as formal N1‐synthons in this modular [5+1] annulation, delivering products with high yields (up to 93%) and diastereoselectivity (generally >10:1 Z:E). The method accommodates diverse substrates and enables late‐stage diversification of sulfonamide pharmaceuticals through a single operation.
Alkynes present unique activation challenges versus allenes and alkenes. Herein, we report a PPh3‐catalyzed double aza‐addition of cyclohexadienone‐tethered alkynoates with sulfonamides to construct densely functionalized cis‐hydrobenzomorpholines through sequential α‐umpolung addition/Michael addition process. Sulfonamides serve as formal N1‐synthons in this modular [5+1] annulation, delivering products with high yields (up to 93%) and diastereoselectivity (generally > 10:1 Z:E). The method accommodates diverse substrates, including drug motifs, enabling late‐stage diversification of sulfonamide pharmaceuticals through a single operation.
item.content:encoded:
A PPh3-catalyzed sequential α-umpolung addition/Michael addition of cyclohexadienone-tethered alkynoates with sulfonamides constructs densely functionalized cis-hydrobenzomorpholines. Sulfonamides serve as formal N1-synthons in this modular [5+1] annulation, delivering products with high yields (up to 93%) and diastereoselectivity (generally >10:1 Z:E). The method accommodates diverse substrates and enables late-stage diversification of sulfonamide pharmaceuticals through a single operation.


Alkynes present unique activation challenges versus allenes and alkenes. Herein, we report a PPh3-catalyzed double aza-addition of cyclohexadienone-tethered alkynoates with sulfonamides to construct densely functionalized cis-hydrobenzomorpholines through sequential α-umpolung addition/Michael addition process. Sulfonamides serve as formal N1-synthons in this modular [5+1] annulation, delivering products with high yields (up to 93%) and diastereoselectivity (generally > 10:1 Z:E). The method accommodates diverse substrates, including drug motifs, enabling late-stage diversification of sulfonamide pharmaceuticals through a single operation.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Samarium Diiodide‐Mediated Radical Borylation of Alkyl Bromides
item.dc:description:
The samarium diiodide‐mediated radical borylation of alkyl bromides has been accomplished. Direct coupling of alkyl bromides and bis(pinacolato)diboranes with samarium diiodide reagents under ligand‐free and base‐free conditions affords a variety of alkyl boronic esters in good to excellent yields.
The samarium diiodide‐mediated radical borylation of alkyl bromides has been accomplished. Direct coupling of alkyl bromides and bis(pinacolato)diboranes with samarium diiodide reagents under ligand‐free and base‐free conditions affords a variety of alkyl boronic esters in good to excellent yields. Broad substrate scope and excellent functional group tolerance have been demonstrated for this radical borylation approach. Mechanistic studies and density functional theory computations have been conducted to gain insight into the reaction pathway for this novel functionalization approach. This general approach is expected to stimulate further progress in the chemistry of lanthanide(II) reagents.
item.content:encoded:

Direct coupling of alkyl bromides and bis(pinacolato)diboranes with samarium diiodide reagents under ligand-free and base-free conditions affords a variety of alkyl boronic esters in good to excellent yields.

Direct coupling of alkyl bromides and bis(pinacolato)diboranes with samarium diiodide reagents under ligand-free and base-free conditions affords a variety of alkyl boronic esters in good to excellent yields. Broad substrate scope and excellent functional group tolerance have been demonstrated for this radical borylation approach. Mechanistic studies and density functional theory computations have been conducted to gain insight into the reaction pathway for this novel functionalization approach. This general approach is expected to stimulate further progress in the chemistry of lanthanide(II) reagents.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Bio‐Photocatalytic Green Synthesis of 2‐Arylbenzoxazoles from Toluene
item.dc:description:
A green and one‐pot synthesis of 2‐arylbenzoxazoles is achieved via a dual‐enzyme photocatalytic cascade. Starting from toluene, the process combines lipase and hemoglobin catalysis in aqueous medium at room temperature, efficiently assembling drug‐like molecules from a simple feedstock with high atom economy and minimal environmental impact.
2‐Arylbenzoxazoles were synthesized efficiently using an innovative, environmentally friendly, and mild system that integrates biocatalysis and photocatalysis. This approach employs sodium anthraquinone sulfonate (SAS) as a photosensitive agent within the same vessel, utilizing water as the solvent. This method effectively converts toluene, an industrial raw material, into the desired final products, which are drug backbone benzoxazole derivatives. The intermediates, benzaldoximes, generated in the initial step are fully utilized in the subsequent stages, and the plausible reaction mechanism is also discussed. This process, along with its satisfactory results, enhances the range of photocatalytic applications, thereby broadening the scope of unnatural reactions catalyzed by two commercially available proteins, porcine pancreas lipase (PPL) and hemoglobin.
item.content:encoded:
A green and one-pot synthesis of 2-arylbenzoxazoles is achieved via a dual-enzyme photocatalytic cascade. Starting from toluene, the process combines lipase and hemoglobin catalysis in aqueous medium at room temperature, efficiently assembling drug-like molecules from a simple feedstock with high atom economy and minimal environmental impact.


2-Arylbenzoxazoles were synthesized efficiently using an innovative, environmentally friendly, and mild system that integrates biocatalysis and photocatalysis. This approach employs sodium anthraquinone sulfonate (SAS) as a photosensitive agent within the same vessel, utilizing water as the solvent. This method effectively converts toluene, an industrial raw material, into the desired final products, which are drug backbone benzoxazole derivatives. The intermediates, benzaldoximes, generated in the initial step are fully utilized in the subsequent stages, and the plausible reaction mechanism is also discussed. This process, along with its satisfactory results, enhances the range of photocatalytic applications, thereby broadening the scope of unnatural reactions catalyzed by two commercially available proteins, porcine pancreas lipase (PPL) and hemoglobin.

item.dc:title: Bio‐Photocatalytic Green Synthesis of 2‐Arylbenzoxazoles from Toluene

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Deep Eutectic Solvent–Mediated Ionic Cascades: Direct Access to Azaindazoles and Related Fused Heterocycles from Diverse N‐Arenes
item.dc:description:
DES‐enabled direct C–H functionalization of N‐heteroarenes generates N‐aryl‐7‐azaindazoles via ylide formation and reaction with aryl diazonium salts using DABCO. An ionic cascade (SN, intramolecular SNAr, electrocyclization, rearomatization) proceeds under mild conditions. This green, safe protocol offers broad scope, supports late‐stage diversification, and affords products with moderate antibacterial activity against resistant Gram‐negative bacteria.
We report a sustainable strategy for the direct C–H functionalization of pyridines, quinolines, isoquinolines, quinoxalines, pyrimidine, and pyrazine to access N‐aryl‐7‐azaindazoles and related fused azaheterocycles. N‐Heteroarene ylides, generated from readily available corresponding precursors, react with aryl diazonium tetrafluoroborates in the presence of DABCO through an ionic cascade of nucleophilic substitution, intramolecular SNAr, electrocyclization, and rearomatization. Deep eutectic solvents (DES) serve as efficient and green media, stabilizing diazonium salts, mitigating safety concerns, and enabling these transformations under mild conditions at 60°C with broad substrate scope. This protocol establishes DES as a safe and environmentally benign platform for heteroarene editing and late‐stage diversification of drug‐like molecules. Downstream functionalization of the final azaindazoles was demonstrated. Some of the final compounds demonstrated moderate antibacterial activity against antibiotic‐resistant gram‐ve bacteria.
item.content:encoded:
DES-enabled direct C–H functionalization of N-heteroarenes generates N-aryl-7-azaindazoles via ylide formation and reaction with aryl diazonium salts using DABCO. An ionic cascade (SN, intramolecular SNAr, electrocyclization, rearomatization) proceeds under mild conditions. This green, safe protocol offers broad scope, supports late-stage diversification, and affords products with moderate antibacterial activity against resistant Gram-negative bacteria.


We report a sustainable strategy for the direct C–H functionalization of pyridines, quinolines, isoquinolines, quinoxalines, pyrimidine, and pyrazine to access N-aryl-7-azaindazoles and related fused azaheterocycles. N-Heteroarene ylides, generated from readily available corresponding precursors, react with aryl diazonium tetrafluoroborates in the presence of DABCO through an ionic cascade of nucleophilic substitution, intramolecular SNAr, electrocyclization, and rearomatization. Deep eutectic solvents (DES) serve as efficient and green media, stabilizing diazonium salts, mitigating safety concerns, and enabling these transformations under mild conditions at 60°C with broad substrate scope. This protocol establishes DES as a safe and environmentally benign platform for heteroarene editing and late-stage diversification of drug-like molecules. Downstream functionalization of the final azaindazoles was demonstrated. Some of the final compounds demonstrated moderate antibacterial activity against antibiotic-resistant gram-ve bacteria.
item.dc:creator: Yashika Tyagi, Subhankar Bera, Soumil Sen, Renu Bhati, Biswajit Guchhait, Dharmarajan Sriram, Tanmay Chatterjee, Ludovic Gremaud, Subhabrata Sen
item.dc:title: Deep Eutectic Solvent–Mediated Ionic Cascades: Direct Access to Azaindazoles and Related Fused Heterocycles from Diverse N‐Arenes

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Exploring Diverse Glycone Preferences of Aspergillus rutinosidases
item.dc:description:
Rutinosidases from Aspergillus niger (AnRut) and A. oryzae (AoRut) catalyze hydrolysis and transglycosylation with non‐natural glycosides as donors, enabling glycosylation of alcoholic and phenolic acceptors. AnRut exhibits broad glycone promiscuity, while AoRut has a narrower substrate scope. Both enzymes produce donor self‐condensation products, expanding the synthetic potential of fungal rutinosidases.
Fungal rutinosidases are retaining diglycosidases that release rutinose from flavonoid glycosides, catalyzing valuable transglycosylation reactions. We systematically compare the substrate specificity and synthetic capabilities of two structurally characterized fungal rutinosidases from Aspergillus niger (AnRut) and A. oryzae (AoRut), across a panel of natural, modified, and non‐natural glycosides. AnRut displays remarkable glycone promiscuity, efficiently hydrolyzing not only rutinosides and β‐glucopyranosides but also β‐galactopyranosides, β‐xylopyranosides, and α‐L‐arabinopyranosides. AnRut also processes diglycosides, such as rutinosides and isomaltosides. It catalyzes transglycosylations with non‐natural glycosides as donors, glycosylating alcoholic and phenolic acceptors, including transfer of β‐D‐galacto‐, β‐D‐xylo‐, and α‐L‐arabinopyranosyl residues. In contrast, AoRut exhibits a narrower substrate range: it recognizes rutinosides and selected monoglycosides as substrates but does not hydrolyze most other diglycosides. Unexpectedly, AoRut catalyzes efficient transglycosylation using p‐nitrophenyl β‐D‐galactopyranoside and pNP‐α‐L‐arabinopyranoside as donors, producing novel β‐galacto‐ and α‐L‐arabinopyranosides of various phenolic acceptors. Both enzymes also catalyze donor self‐condensation products, such as pNP‐β‐D‐glucopyranosyl‐(1→3)‐β‐D‐glucopyranoside and pNP‐β‐D‐xylopyranosyl‐(1→3)‐β‐D‐xylopyranoside and pNP‐β‐D‐xylopyranosyl‐(1→2)‐β‐D‐xylopyranoside (only AoRut). Notably, this is the first reported case of a rutinosidase glycosylating a sugar acceptor. These findings reveal distinct glycone preferences and mechanistic features of the two enzymes and significantly expand the repertoire of glycosides accessible through fungal rutinosidases, highlighting their utility for synthesizing novel carbohydrate derivatives.
item.content:encoded:
Rutinosidases from Aspergillus niger (AnRut) and A. oryzae (AoRut) catalyze hydrolysis and transglycosylation with non-natural glycosides as donors, enabling glycosylation of alcoholic and phenolic acceptors. AnRut exhibits broad glycone promiscuity, while AoRut has a narrower substrate scope. Both enzymes produce donor self-condensation products, expanding the synthetic potential of fungal rutinosidases.


Fungal rutinosidases are retaining diglycosidases that release rutinose from flavonoid glycosides, catalyzing valuable transglycosylation reactions. We systematically compare the substrate specificity and synthetic capabilities of two structurally characterized fungal rutinosidases from Aspergillus niger (AnRut) and A. oryzae (AoRut), across a panel of natural, modified, and non-natural glycosides. AnRut displays remarkable glycone promiscuity, efficiently hydrolyzing not only rutinosides and β-glucopyranosides but also β-galactopyranosides, β-xylopyranosides, and α-L-arabinopyranosides. AnRut also processes diglycosides, such as rutinosides and isomaltosides. It catalyzes transglycosylations with non-natural glycosides as donors, glycosylating alcoholic and phenolic acceptors, including transfer of β-D-galacto-, β-D-xylo-, and α-L-arabinopyranosyl residues. In contrast, AoRut exhibits a narrower substrate range: it recognizes rutinosides and selected monoglycosides as substrates but does not hydrolyze most other diglycosides. Unexpectedly, AoRut catalyzes efficient transglycosylation using p-nitrophenyl β-D-galactopyranoside and pNP-α-L-arabinopyranoside as donors, producing novel β-galacto- and α-L-arabinopyranosides of various phenolic acceptors. Both enzymes also catalyze donor self-condensation products, such as pNP-β-D-glucopyranosyl-(1→3)-β-D-glucopyranoside and pNP-β-D-xylopyranosyl-(1→3)-β-D-xylopyranoside and pNP-β-D-xylopyranosyl-(1→2)-β-D-xylopyranoside (only AoRut). item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Gold(I)‐Catalyzed C(sp3)–C(sp) Cross‐Coupling: Access to Alkynylated Tetrahydrofuran Derivatives
item.dc:description:
An Au(I)‐catalyzed C(sp3)–C(sp) cross‐coupling, unsuccessful under photosensitized conditions for vinylgold(I) complexes, is achieved using MeDalPhosAuCl under thermal conditions, giving alkynylated tetrahydrofuran derivatives. The reaction proceeds via an Au(III) intermediate formed by oxidative addition of an alkynyl iodide.
This study explores the possibility of operating a previously unsuccessful Au(I)‐catalyzed C(sp3)–C(sp) cross‐coupling. While the photosensitization conditions previously used for the C(sp2)–C(sp) cross‐coupling reaction with vinylgold(I) intermediates were not successful with alkylgold(I) complexes, the use of the MeDalPhosAuCl complex as a catalyst under thermal conditions provided moderate to good yields of C(sp3)‐alkynylated adducts, particularly alkynylated tetrahydrofuran derivatives. Mechanistic studies revealed the formation of an Au(III) intermediate, resulting from oxidative addition of the alkynyl iodide partner onto the Au(I) complex bearing the hemilabile MeDalPhos ligand.

The reaction proceeds via an Au(III) intermediate formed by oxidative addition of an alkynyl iodide.


This study explores the possibility of operating a previously unsuccessful Au(I)-catalyzed C(sp3)–C(sp) cross-coupling. While the photosensitization conditions previously used for the C(sp2)–C(sp) cross-coupling reaction with vinylgold(I) intermediates were not successful with alkylgold(I) complexes, the use of the MeDalPhosAuCl complex as a catalyst under thermal conditions provided moderate to good yields of C(sp3)-alkynylated adducts, particularly alkynylated tetrahydrofuran derivatives. Mechanistic studies revealed the formation of an Au(III) intermediate, resulting from oxidative addition of the alkynyl iodide partner onto the Au(I) complex bearing the hemilabile MeDalPhos ligand.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Copper‐Catalyzed Regio‐ and Enantioselective Hydroallylation of 1‐SeCF3‐Alkenes
item.dc:description:
A copper‐catalyzed regio‐ and enantioselective hydroallylation of 1‐SeCF3‐alkenes has been developed. Using a chiral CuH catalyst, asymmetric C(sp3)─C(sp3) bond formation at the α‐position to a SeCF3 group was achieved for the first time. The product enantiopurity highly depended on the leaving group of the allylic electrophile employed, with allylic bromides providing superior enantioselectivity.
A copper‐catalyzed regio‐ and enantioselective hydroallylation of 1‐trifluoromethylseleno (SeCF3)‐alkenes with hydrosilanes and allylic halides has been developed. The in situ generated copper hydride catalyst enabled access to untrivial aliphatic SeCF3‐containing molecules, including substrates derived from bioactive scaffolds. Furthermore, the use of a chiral (S)‐H8‐BINAP ligand successfully induced point chirality at the α‐SeCF3 carbon center. The enantiopurity of the products was found to be highly dependent on the leaving group of the allylic electrophile employed, with allylic bromides affording significantly enhanced enantioselectivity. To the best of our knowledge, this transformation represents the first example of asymmetric C(sp3)─C(sp3) bond formation at the α‐position to a SeCF3 group, a structurally underexplored yet potentially valuable motif in drug design.
item.content:encoded:
A copper-catalyzed regio- and enantioselective hydroallylation of 1-SeCF3-alkenes has been developed. Using a chiral CuH catalyst, asymmetric C(sp3)─C(sp3) bond formation at the α-position to a SeCF3 group was achieved for the first time. The product enantiopurity highly depended on the leaving group of the allylic electrophile employed, with allylic bromides providing superior enantioselectivity.


A copper-catalyzed regio- and enantioselective hydroallylation of 1-trifluoromethylseleno (SeCF3)-alkenes with hydrosilanes and allylic halides has been developed. The in situ generated copper hydride catalyst enabled access to untrivial aliphatic SeCF3-containing molecules, including substrates derived from bioactive scaffolds. Furthermore, the use of a chiral (S)-H8-BINAP ligand successfully induced point chirality at the α-SeCF3 carbon center. The enantiopurity of the products was found to be highly dependent on the leaving group of the allylic electrophile employed, with allylic bromides affording significantly enhanced enantioselectivity. To the best of our knowledge, this transformation represents the first example of asymmetric C(sp3)─C(sp3) bond formation at the α-position to a SeCF3 group, a structurally underexplored yet potentially valuable motif in drug design.

item.dc:title: Copper‐Catalyzed Regio‐ and Enantioselective Hydroallylation of 1‐SeCF3‐Alkenes

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesis of Double‐Thio‐Functionalized Pentasubstituted Pyridines Using α‐Enemalononitrile Ketene Dithioacetals and Thiophenols and Their Selective Arylthio Substitution Reactions
item.dc:description:
An addition/cyclization reaction between α‐enemalononitrile ketene dithioacetal and thiophenols generates double‐thio‐functionalized pentasubstituted pyridines rapidly. This reaction occurs under mild conditions without the use of oxidants or transition metals, offering a wide substrate scope and excellent functional group tolerance. Furthermore, the resulting products readily undergo selective aromatic thio substitution reactions.
We report an addition/cyclization reaction between α‐enemalononitrile ketene dithioacetal and thiophenols that rapidly generates double‐thio‐functionalized pentasubstituted pyridines. This reaction proceeds under mild conditions without oxidants or transition metals, offering broad substrate scope and excellent functional group tolerance. Additionally, the products can easily undergo selective aromatic thio substitution reactions. The reaction mechanism is likely to involve the formation of an electron‐donor–acceptor (EDA) complex.
item.content:encoded:
An addition/cyclization reaction between α-enemalononitrile ketene dithioacetal and thiophenols generates double-thio-functionalized pentasubstituted pyridines rapidly. This reaction occurs under mild conditions without the use of oxidants or transition metals, offering a wide substrate scope and excellent functional group tolerance. Furthermore, the resulting products readily undergo selective aromatic thio substitution reactions.


We report an addition/cyclization reaction between α-enemalononitrile ketene dithioacetal and thiophenols that rapidly generates double-thio-functionalized pentasubstituted pyridines. This reaction proceeds under mild conditions without oxidants or transition metals, offering broad substrate scope and excellent functional group tolerance. Additionally, the products can easily undergo selective aromatic thio substitution reactions. The reaction mechanism is likely to involve the formation of an electron-donor–acceptor (EDA) complex.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Visible Light‐Induced Semi‐Reduction of Azo‐Compounds into Hydrazines
item.dc:description:
A visible light–induced semi‐hydrogenation method has been developed to afford hydrazines exclusively, with a broad substrate scope and excellent functional group tolerance. This method provides a novel approach for synthesizing various hydrazines, while minimizing aniline byproducts to achieve high chemoselectivity. The generation of the highly reducing species TX˙− facilitates two stepwise single‐electron reductions, thereby yielding key intermediates.
The semi‐hydrogenation of azo‐compounds to hydrazines enables important applications, including dye decolorization, pharmaceutical synthesis, among others. The primary hurdle of this reaction is minimizing aniline byproducts to achieve high chemoselectivity. To address this challenge, a visible light–induced semi‐hydrogenation was developed, affording hydrazines exclusively, with a broad substrate scope and excellent functional group tolerance. The generation of highly reducing species TX˙− facilitates two stepwise single‐electron reductions, thereby yielding a N‐centered radical and then an amine anion as the key intermediates.

The generation of the highly reducing species TX˙− facilitates two stepwise single-electron reductions, thereby yielding key intermediates.
