The primary hurdle of this reaction is minimizing aniline byproducts to achieve high chemoselectivity. To address this challenge, a visible light–induced semi-hydrogenation was developed, affording hydrazines exclusively, with a broad substrate scope and excellent functional group tolerance. The generation of highly reducing species TX˙ − facilitates two stepwise single-electron reductions, thereby yielding a N-centered radical and then an amine anion as the key intermediates.

item.title: Catalyst‐Free, O2‐Mediated C(sp2)H Alkylation of Azauracils With Alkylboronic Acids
item.dc:description:
The O2‐mediated radical alkylation of azauracils via the autoxidation of alkylboronic acids was developed, providing a variety of C6‐alkylated azauracils in yields up to 90% under catalyst‐free conditions.
The O2‐mediated radical alkylation of azauracils via the autoxidation of alkylboronic acids was developed under mild reaction conditions. In this transformation, O2 was employed as sole oxidant to access a variety of C6‐alkylated azauracils in yields up to 90% under catalyst‐free conditions. This reaction features with broad substrate scope, good functional group tolerance and easy operation.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Counterion Effects Enable Divergent Pd‐Catalyzed Carbonylative Cyclization of Amide‐Tethered o‐Alkynylphenols
item.dc:description: The catalytic carbonylation of alkynes is a powerful route to heteroaromatic scaffolds, yet achieving regioselectivity remains challenging. Herein, we report a divergent carbonylative cyclization of 2‐alkynylphenol derivatives that affords either coumarins (6‐endo‐dig) or 2‐coumaranones (5‐exo‐dig) by tuning the palladium‐based catalytic system. Pd(OAc)2 in combination with TFA favored coumarins formation with high yield and selectivity (>15:1), whereas electron‐deficient species such as Pd(MeCN)4(BF4)2 promoted 2‐coumaranones with up to 84% yield and >25:1 selectivity. While many approaches exploit ligand design to steer selectivity, these results highlight counterion effects as a powerful tool to control regioselectivity in palladium‐catalyzed carbonylative cyclization, expanding the utility of alkyne carbonylation for the synthesis of biologically relevant heterocycles.

Herein, we report a divergent carbonylative cyclization of 2-alkynylphenol derivatives that affords either coumarins (6-endo-dig) or 2-coumaranones (5-exo-dig) by tuning the palladium-based catalytic system. Pd(OAc)2 in combination with TFA favored coumarins formation with high yield and selectivity (>15:1), whereas electron-deficient species such as Pd(MeCN)4(BF4)2 promoted 2-coumaranones with up to 84% yield and >25:1 selectivity. While many approaches exploit ligand design to steer selectivity, these results highlight counterion effects as a powerful tool to control regioselectivity in palladium-catalyzed carbonylative cyclization, expanding the utility of alkyne carbonylation for the synthesis of biologically relevant heterocycles.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Lighting the Path to Cyclic Phosphonamides via Halogen‐Bond Electron Donor–Acceptor Activation
item.dc:description:
Cyclic phosphinamides are valuable scaffolds for optoelectronic applications, yet remain difficult to access with existing methods. Here, we highlight a mild and straightforward visible light‐driven strategy that enables the formation of cyclic phosphinamides and thiophosphinamides.
Phosphinamide‐based scaffolds and related P‐heterocycles emerge as valuable scaffolds in optoelectronics, particularly OLED technologies, as well as in asymmetric catalysis. However, access to cyclic phosphinamides remains synthetically challenging and poorly described, limiting exploration of their full potential. We report here an efficient, mild, metal‐free, and photocatalyst‐free photoinduced strategy that expands the synthetic toolbox to reach cyclic phosphinamides and thiophosphinamides. This approach relies on halogen‐bond‐assisted electron donor–acceptor (HBA‐EDA) complex formation to trigger single‐electron transfer and promote efficient CC bond construction under gentle conditions. Comprehensive mechanistic studies, including UV, radical quenching, EPR (electron paramagnetic resonance), and DFT (Density functional theory) analyses, confirm the involvement of carbon‐ and phosphorus‐centered radicals and elucidate key intermediates, including a rearrangement process that defines two operative mechanistic pathways. This method provides a straightforward and sustainable way to access new phosphorus‐containing compounds with potential for future optoelectronic applications, as well as new insights into HBA‐EDA mechanism.
item.content:encoded:
Cyclic phosphinamides are valuable scaffolds for optoelectronic applications, yet remain difficult to access with existing methods. Here, we highlight a mild and straightforward visible light-driven strategy that enables the formation of cyclic phosphinamides and thiophosphinamides.

However, access to cyclic phosphinamides remains synthetically challenging and poorly described, limiting exploration of their full potential. We report here an efficient, mild, metal-free, and photocatalyst-free photoinduced strategy that expands the synthetic toolbox to reach cyclic phosphinamides and thiophosphinamides. This approach relies on halogen-bond-assisted electron donor–acceptor (HBA-EDA) complex formation to trigger single-electron transfer and promote efficient CC bond construction under gentle conditions. Comprehensive mechanistic studies, including UV, radical quenching, EPR (electron paramagnetic resonance), and DFT (Density functional theory) analyses, confirm the involvement of carbon- and phosphorus-centered radicals and elucidate key intermediates, including a rearrangement process that defines two operative mechanistic pathways. This method provides a straightforward and sustainable way to access new phosphorus-containing compounds with potential for future optoelectronic applications, as well as new insights into HBA-EDA mechanism.

item.dc:title: Lighting the Path to Cyclic Phosphonamides via Halogen‐Bond Electron Donor–Acceptor Activation

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Annulation of 1,2,3‐Thiadiazoles and Alkynes to Construct Thiophene Derivatives Enabled by Visible‐Light Photocatalysis
item.dc:description:
A visible‐light photocatalytic denitrogenative annulation of 1,2,3‐thiadiazoles with alkynes is developed to synthesize thiophene derivatives.
Although the transition‐metal‐catalyzed denitrogenative annulation of 1,2,3‐thiadiazoles has been greatly advanced over the past decade, visible‐light‐mediated conversion of 1,2,3‐thiadiazoles remains rarely explored. Herein, a mild and efficient strategy for the synthesis of thiophene derivatives via visible‐light photocatalytic denitrogenative annulation of 1,2,3‐thiadiazoles with alkynes is achieved. This protocol shows broad substrate scope with good selectivity. Combined experimental and computational mechanistic studies reveal a feasible reaction pathway initiated by photocatalyst‐mediated energy transfer to the 1,2,3‐thiadiazole, followed by sequential denitrogenation to generate a key 1,3‐diradical intermediate. This intermediate could undergo regioselective addition to the alkyne and subsequent ring closure to afford the desired products. This work expands the synthetic utility of visible‐light photocatalysis in the denitrogenative functionalization of 1,2,3‐thiadiazoles.
item.content:encoded:
A visible-light photocatalytic denitrogenative annulation of 1,2,3-thiadiazoles with alkynes is developed to synthesize thiophene derivatives.


Although the transition-metal-catalyzed denitrogenative annulation of 1,2,3-thiadiazoles has been greatly advanced over the past decade, visible-light-mediated conversion of 1,2,3-thiadiazoles remains rarely explored. Herein, a mild and efficient strategy for the synthesis of thiophene derivatives via visible-light photocatalytic denitrogenative annulation of 1,2,3-thiadiazoles with alkynes is achieved. This protocol shows broad substrate scope with good selectivity. Combined experimental and computational mechanistic studies reveal a feasible reaction pathway initiated by photocatalyst-mediated energy transfer to the 1,2,3-thiadiazole, followed by sequential denitrogenation to generate a key 1,3-diradical intermediate. This intermediate could undergo regioselective addition to the alkyne and subsequent ring closure to afford the desired products. This work expands the synthetic utility of visible-light photocatalysis in the denitrogenative functionalization of 1,2,3-thiadiazoles.

item.dc:title: Annulation of 1,2,3‐Thiadiazoles and Alkynes to Construct Thiophene Derivatives Enabled by Visible‐Light Photocatalysis

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Ni‐Based Metal Cavities‐Mediated Ensemble Effect for Catalytic Hydroconversion of Lignin into Aromatic Monomers
item.dc:description:
Via an ensemble effect, Ni‐based metal cavities promote electron transfer from carbon black to Niδ+ to enhance H2 activation to H*, while π–π interaction between the carbon black support and lignin aromatic rings suppresses over‐hydrogenation, achieving 81.0% lignin conversion and 45.5 wt.% aromatic monomers yield.
An efficient catalytic hydroconversion of lignin into aromatic monomers (AMs) is not only critical for the economic viability of lignin refining, but also reduces dependence on fossil fuels. Nevertheless, current molecular‐level studies on H2 activation in catalytic hydroconversion of lignin are primarily focused on single‐atom catalysts (SACs) or nanoparticle catalysts (NPCs), leaving the H2 activation capabilities of metal cavities largely unexplored. Herein, we report the construction of a composite material featuring a multi‐Ni‐atom metal cavities for the catalytic hydroconversion of lignin. This study employs a dual‐induction mechanism to synthesize multi‐Ni‐atom metal cavity catalysts exhibiting an ensemble effect: Initially, hydrogen bonding mediates the increase in intermolecular size of the Ni species precursor; subsequently, high‐temperature thermal induction generates multi‐Ni‐atom metal cavities with the ensemble effect. In contrast to SACs or NPCs, these metal cavity structures promote efficient electron transfer between the graphitic carbon black support and the Niδ+ species via an ensemble effect, thereby enhancing the intrinsic ability of Ni species to activate H2 into H*. Moreover, the π–π interaction between the electron‐deficient graphitized carbon black support and electron‐rich aromatic rings (ARs) in lignin promotes the fixation of ARs, suppressing their over‐hydrogenation. This leads to a lignin conversion rate of 81.0% and an AM yield of 45.5 wt.%, demonstrating excellent ensemble catalytic performance. The Ni‐based metal cavity catalysts with the ensemble effect constructed in this study provide theoretical support for obtaining lignin‐derived small‐molecule platform compounds.
item.content:encoded:

An efficient catalytic hydroconversion of lignin into aromatic monomers (AMs) is not only critical for the economic viability of lignin refining, but also reduces dependence on fossil fuels. Nevertheless, current molecular-level studies on H2 activation in catalytic hydroconversion of lignin are primarily focused on single-atom catalysts (SACs) or nanoparticle catalysts (NPCs), leaving the H2 activation capabilities of metal cavities largely unexplored. Herein, we report the construction of a composite material featuring a multi-Ni-atom metal cavities for the catalytic hydroconversion of lignin. This study employs a dual-induction mechanism to synthesize multi-Ni-atom metal cavity catalysts exhibiting an ensemble effect: Initially, hydrogen bonding mediates the increase in intermolecular size of the Ni species precursor; subsequently, high-temperature thermal induction generates multi-Ni-atom metal cavities with the ensemble effect. In contrast to SACs or NPCs, these metal cavity structures promote efficient electron transfer between the graphitic carbon black support and the Niδ+ species via an ensemble effect, thereby enhancing the intrinsic ability of Ni species to activate H2 into H*. Moreover, the π–π interaction between the electron-deficient graphitized carbon black support and electron-rich aromatic rings (ARs) in lignin promotes the fixation of ARs, suppressing their over-hydrogenation. This leads to a lignin conversion rate of 81.0% and an AM yield of 45.5 wt.%, demonstrating excellent ensemble catalytic performance. The Ni-based metal cavity catalysts with the ensemble effect constructed in this study provide theoretical support for obtaining lignin-derived small-molecule platform compounds.
item.dc:creator: Zhong‐Qiu Liu, Shi‐Qi Jin, Qing–Qing Sun, Yu‐Jing Liu, An‐Guo Ying, Long‐Yu Zhang, Xing‐Shun Cong, Mei‐Xia Zhao, Xiao‐Long Zhang
item.dc:title: Ni‐Based Metal Cavities‐Mediated Ensemble Effect for Catalytic Hydroconversion of Lignin into Aromatic Monomers

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Photoinduced Amine‐Mediated Alkylpyridylation of Styrenes
item.dc:description:
A photoinduced alkylpyridylation of styrenes has been developed under catalyst‐free conditions using alkyl iodides and substituted 4‐cyanopyridines as a source of alkyl and pyridyl precursors, respectively. Mechanistic and DFT studies suggest that the amine plays a crucial role in generating the corresponding alkyl radical and aryl radical anion via halogen bonding and an EDA‐initiated SET mechanism, respectively.
Herein, we report the photoinduced, catalyst‐free alkylpyridylation of styrenes to access privileged 1,1‐diarylalkane scaffolds. Unactivated alkyl iodides and substituted 4‐cyanopyridines serve as the alkyl and pyridyl precursors, respectively. Preliminary mechanistic and computational studies suggest that the amine plays a crucial role in generating the corresponding alkyl radical and aryl radical anion, possibly via halogen bonding and EDA‐initiated SET mechanisms, respectively. The method offers several advantages, including mild reaction conditions, wide substrate compatibility, and suitability for late‐stage modification of pharmaceutical compounds.
item.content:encoded:

Mechanistic and DFT studies suggest that the amine plays a crucial role in generating the corresponding alkyl radical and aryl radical anion via halogen bonding and an EDA-initiated SET mechanism, respectively.


Herein, we report the photoinduced, catalyst-free alkylpyridylation of styrenes to access privileged 1,1-diarylalkane scaffolds. Unactivated alkyl iodides and substituted 4-cyanopyridines serve as the alkyl and pyridyl precursors, respectively. Preliminary mechanistic and computational studies suggest that the amine plays a crucial role in generating the corresponding alkyl radical and aryl radical anion, possibly via halogen bonding and EDA-initiated SET mechanisms, respectively. The method offers several advantages, including mild reaction conditions, wide substrate compatibility, and suitability for late-stage modification of pharmaceutical compounds.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Bridging Liquid and Gas Phase Catalysis for Abiotic CO2 Conversion to Prebiotic Organics Over Mesoporous Silica‐Supported Ni–Co Catalysts
item.dc:description:
Under simulated hydrothermal vent conditions, Ni⋅Co bimetallic catalysts supported on silica convert CO2 and H2O into key metabolites such as formate, methanol, and ethanol. Water plays a critical role in CO2 adsorption and product selectivity, shaping catalytic performance across both liquid and gas phases.
Reduced carbon species such as formate and methane are widely observed in serpentinizing hydrothermal systems, suggesting that abiotic CO2 hydrogenation can proceed on native metal surfaces or mineral‐supported catalysts. Here, we systematically evaluate SBA‐15 mesoporous silica‐supported Ni–Co catalysts as model systems for the abiotic conversion of CO2 to prebiotic organic molecules under both liquid‐ and gas‐phase conditions. Under liquid‐phase conditions (25°C, 63 bar), all catalysts promote CO2 reduction to formate and ethanol at millimolar concentrations using water as the hydrogen source, while the addition of molecular H2 enhances formate formation at the expense of ethanol production. In contrast, gas‐phase reactions (230–270°C, 20 bar) favor CO2 conversion to CO, methanol, methane, and ethane. Across both phases, catalytic activity and product selectivity exhibit a pronounced dependence on the Ni⋅Co composition, highlighting the critical role of bimetallic interactions. Notably, water strongly modulates CO2 adsorption and the resulting product distribution, underscoring its dual function as both solvent and reactive species. These findings provide insight into phase‐dependent CO2 hydrogenation pathways over Ni⋅Co catalysts and offer a catalytic framework for understanding abiotic routes to prebiotic carbon compounds.
item.content:encoded:
Under simulated hydrothermal vent conditions, Ni⋅Co bimetallic catalysts supported on silica convert CO2 and H2O into key metabolites such as formate, methanol, and ethanol. Water plays a critical role in CO2 adsorption and product selectivity, shaping catalytic performance across both liquid and gas phases.


Reduced carbon species such as formate and methane are widely observed in serpentinizing hydrothermal systems, suggesting that abiotic CO2 hydrogenation can proceed on native metal surfaces or mineral-supported catalysts. Here, we systematically evaluate SBA-15 mesoporous silica-supported Ni–Co catalysts as model systems for the abiotic conversion of CO2 to prebiotic organic molecules under both liquid- and gas-phase conditions. Under liquid-phase conditions (25°C, 63 bar), all catalysts promote CO2 reduction to formate and ethanol at millimolar concentrations using water as the hydrogen source, while the addition of molecular H2 enhances formate formation at the expense of ethanol production. In contrast, gas-phase reactions (230–270°C, 20 bar) favor CO2 conversion to CO, methanol, methane, and ethane. Across both phases, catalytic activity and product selectivity exhibit a pronounced dependence on the Ni⋅Co composition, highlighting the critical role of bimetallic interactions. Notably, water strongly modulates CO2 adsorption and the resulting product distribution, underscoring its dual function as both solvent and reactive species. These findings provide insight into phase-dependent CO2 hydrogenation pathways over Ni⋅Co catalysts and offer a catalytic framework for understanding abiotic routes to prebiotic carbon compounds.

item.dc:title: Bridging Liquid and Gas Phase Catalysis for Abiotic CO2 Conversion to Prebiotic Organics Over Mesoporous Silica‐Supported Ni–Co Catalysts

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Enantioselective Tandem Povarov/Urech–Read Reaction for the Synthesis of Dihydroquinoline/Hydantoin Hybrids
item.dc:description:
Herein, an organocatalytic asymmetric synthesis of dihydroquinoline/hydantoin hybrids is described. The synthetic protocol involves a tandem reaction comprising a phosphoric acid–catalyzed initial Povarov dimerization of in situ generated imine/enamine mixtures under tautomeric equilibrium, followed by a Urech–Read reaction of the 1,2‐dihydroquinoline intermediates, which are amino acid derivatives.
Herein, an organocatalytic asymmetric synthesis of dihydroquinoline/hydantoin hybrids is described. The synthetic protocol involves a tandem reaction comprising a phosphoric acid–catalyzed initial Povarov dimerization of in situ generated imine/enamine mixtures under tautomeric equilibrium, followed by a Urech–Read reaction of the 1,2‐dihydroquinoline intermediates, which are amino acid derivatives.

The synthetic protocol involves a tandem reaction comprising a phosphoric acid–catalyzed initial Povarov dimerization of in situ generated imine/enamine mixtures under tautomeric equilibrium, followed by a Urech–Read reaction of the 1,2-dihydroquinoline intermediates, which are amino acid derivatives.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Synergetic Bifunctional Effect of the Solvent Leading to External Base‐Free Neutral Construction of Unsymmetrical Thioethers From Thiols and Organohalides in N,N‐Dimethylformamide
item.dc:description:
Without adding any external base, solvent DMF is found able to effectively promote the substitution reactions of thiols and various organohalides to afford the useful unsymmetrical thioethers under neutral and greener conditions. Studies show that DMF may have a synergetic bifunctional effect in the reactions working as both the best solvent and an excellent organic base.
Different to other solvents, a unique promoting effect of DMF is observed in the aromatic nucleophilic substitution reaction of heteroaryl halides and thiols. Thus, by using DMF as the solvent, a greener method can be developed for direct neutral synthesis of heteroaryl thioethers without adding any external base into the reaction. This method can also be extended to alkyl and electron‐deficient aryl halides, providing a new way for external base‐free neutral construction of the useful unsymmetrical thioethers. Control reactions revealed that DMF functions more than a good solvent in the reaction, i.e., a synergetic bifunctional effect of DMF working as both the best solvent and an excellent organic base can eventually promote the substitution reactions of thiols and organohalides effectively without using any external base.
item.content:encoded:

Studies show that DMF may have a synergetic bifunctional effect in the reactions working as both the best solvent and an excellent organic base.


Different to other solvents, a unique promoting effect of DMF is observed in the aromatic nucleophilic substitution reaction of heteroaryl halides and thiols. Thus, by using DMF as the solvent, a greener method can be developed for direct neutral synthesis of heteroaryl thioethers without adding any external base into the reaction. This method can also be extended to alkyl and electron-deficient aryl halides, providing a new way for external base-free neutral construction of the useful unsymmetrical thioethers. Control reactions revealed that DMF functions more than a good solvent in the reaction, i.e., a synergetic bifunctional effect of DMF working as both the best solvent and an excellent organic base can eventually promote the substitution reactions of thiols and organohalides effectively without using any external base.

item.dc:title: Synergetic Bifunctional Effect of the Solvent Leading to External Base‐Free Neutral Construction of Unsymmetrical Thioethers From Thiols and Organohalides in N,N‐Dimethylformamide

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Guiding Carriers and Steering Spatial Separation via Defect‐Coupled Non‐precious Co‐catalysts for Boosted Photocatalytic H2O2 Production
item.dc:description: Artificial photosynthesis offers a promising strategy for the sustainable production of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). However, the practical application of conventional single‐component photocatalysts is often constrained by intrinsic limitations, such as inefficient charge‐carrier separation and transport, which ultimately restrict their overall photocatalytic performance. In this work, we synthesized sulfur vacancy‐engineered CdS (denoted as CdSv) and constructed a non‐noble metal heterojunction by integrating CdSv with MXene cocatalysts through electrostatic self‐assembly (denoted as: CdSvTC). Under sacrificial agent‐free conditions, the CdSvTC catalyst achieves a high photocatalytic H2O2 production rate of 77.1 μmol·g−1·min−1 via a dual‐reaction pathway, primarily driven by the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) with auxiliary contribution from the water oxidation reaction (WOR). In situ and ex situ X‐ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterizations reveal that the enhanced activity originates from the synergistic effects of two mechanisms: trap states created by the introduced S‐vacancies improve the separation and guidance of photogenerated carriers, while MXene cocatalyst enables steering spatial separation and suppresses charge recombination, thereby collectively facilitating the photocatalytic oxygen reduction process. In situ attenuated total reflectance surface‐enhanced infrared absorption spectroscopy (ATR‐SEIRAS) confirms stabilized oxygenated species on the catalyst surface. Moreover, the CdSvTC sample exhibits an outstanding light‐harvesting capability and high performance under natural solar irradiation and ambient atmosphere, demonstrating great potential for practical application. This study provides a valuable reference for the future design of efficient non‐noble metal heterojunction photocatalysts.
item.content:encoded: Artificial photosynthesis offers a promising strategy for the sustainable production of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). However, the practical application of conventional single-component photocatalysts is often constrained by intrinsic limitations, such as inefficient charge-carrier separation and transport, which ultimately restrict their overall photocatalytic performance. In this work, we synthesized sulfur vacancy-engineered CdS (denoted as CdSv) and constructed a non-noble metal heterojunction by integrating CdSv with MXene cocatalysts through electrostatic self-assembly (denoted as: CdSvTC). Under sacrificial agent-free conditions, the CdSvTC catalyst achieves a high photocatalytic H2O2 production rate of 77.1 μmol·g−1·min−1 via a dual-reaction pathway, primarily driven by the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) with auxiliary contribution from the water oxidation reaction (WOR). In situ and ex situ X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterizations reveal that the enhanced activity originates from the synergistic effects of two mechanisms: trap states created by the introduced S-vacancies improve the separation and guidance of photogenerated carriers, while MXene cocatalyst enables steering spatial separation and suppresses charge recombination, thereby collectively facilitating the photocatalytic oxygen reduction process. In situ attenuated total reflectance surface-enhanced infrared absorption spectroscopy (ATR-SEIRAS) confirms stabilized oxygenated species on the catalyst surface. Moreover, the CdSvTC sample exhibits an outstanding light-harvesting capability and high performance under natural solar irradiation and ambient atmosphere, demonstrating great potential for practical application. This study provides a valuable reference for the future design of efficient non-noble metal heterojunction photocatalysts.

item.dc:title: Guiding Carriers and Steering Spatial Separation via Defect‐Coupled Non‐precious Co‐catalysts for Boosted Photocatalytic H2O2 Production

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Photochemical Multicomponent Decarboxylative Radical Coupling for the Synthesis of Imidazolidine Derivatives
item.dc:description: The 1,2,3,4‐tetraphenyl‐oxazolium cation (TPO+) is widely utilized as a room‐temperature phosphorescent molecule and anion sensor owing to its ability to exhibit aggregation‐induced emission. However, its capability as a photocatalyst has never been examined. In this report, the positively charged core in TPO+ was shown to be effective at photooxidizing substrate molecules, making it a potent photocatalyst. A series of symmetrical and unsymmetrical imidazolidine derivatives was prepared at room temperature by a photocatalytic three‐component coupling protocol. A series of photophysical studies, including Stern–Volmer analysis of excited‐state quenching and successful interception of intermediate radicals, delineate the mechanism of this coupling reaction. Such an organocatalytic pathway, along with the mild reaction conditions, represents a significant improvement over prior methods for the synthesis of imidazolidines.
item.content:encoded: The 1,2,3,4-tetraphenyl-oxazolium cation (TPO+) is widely utilized as a room-temperature phosphorescent molecule and anion sensor owing to its ability to exhibit aggregation-induced emission. However, its capability as a photocatalyst has never been examined. In this report, the positively charged core in TPO+ was shown to be effective at photooxidizing substrate molecules, making it a potent photocatalyst. A series of symmetrical and unsymmetrical imidazolidine derivatives was prepared at room temperature by a photocatalytic three-component coupling protocol. A series of photophysical studies, including Stern–Volmer analysis of excited-state quenching and successful interception of intermediate radicals, delineate the mechanism of this coupling reaction. Such an organocatalytic pathway, along with the mild reaction conditions, represents a significant improvement over prior methods for the synthesis of imidazolidines.

item.dc:title: Photochemical Multicomponent Decarboxylative Radical Coupling for the Synthesis of Imidazolidine Derivatives

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Decoding Transmetalation in Miyaura Borylation: The Hidden Chemistry of Bis(pinacolato) Diboron and Base Effects
item.dc:description:
DFT analysis of the Pd(IMesMe)(cin)Cl precatalyst in Miyaura borylation of aryl chlorides clarifies key mechanistic steps. The second base equivalent directly participates, while B2Pin2 activation involves Pd coordination rather than independent cleavage. A concerted oxidative addition–transmetalation pathway is revealed, redefining the mechanism and suggesting milder conditions and similar behavior in other cross‐couplings.
We present a comprehensive mechanistic investigation of the well‐defined precatalyst [Pd(IMesMe)(cin)Cl], which has exhibited high catalytic efficiency in the Miyaura borylation of various aryl chlorides. Despite its proven catalytic performance, key aspects of the mechanism, particularly the transmetalation step, remained elusive. In this study, we addressed these mechanistic aspects through detailed density functional theory (DFT) calculations. Our results elucidate the role of the second equivalent of base, pinpointing its specific involvement within the catalytic cycle. Furthermore, we unraveled the activation pathway of bis(pinacolato)diboron (B2Pin2), determining whether base‐mediated cleavage occurs independently or requires direct coordination to the palladium center. The mechanism reveals a surprising concerted fusion of the oxidative addition and transmetalation steps, paving the way not only for a new avenue in the Miyaura reaction, but potentially for other cross‐coupling processes. This synergy may prompt a re‐evaluation of previous studies, suggesting that similar pathways could enable milder reaction conditions.
item.content:encoded:
DFT analysis of the Pd(IMesMe)(cin)Cl precatalyst in Miyaura borylation of aryl chlorides clarifies key mechanistic steps. The second base equivalent directly participates, while B2Pin2 activation involves Pd coordination rather than independent cleavage. A concerted oxidative addition–transmetalation pathway is revealed, redefining the mechanism and suggesting milder conditions and similar behavior in other cross-couplings.


We present a comprehensive mechanistic investigation of the well-defined precatalyst [Pd(IMesMe)(cin)Cl], which has exhibited high catalytic efficiency in the Miyaura borylation of various aryl chlorides. Despite its proven catalytic performance, key aspects of the mechanism, particularly the transmetalation step, remained elusive. In this study, we addressed these mechanistic aspects through detailed density functional theory (DFT) calculations. Our results elucidate the role of the second equivalent of base, pinpointing its specific involvement within the catalytic cycle. Furthermore, we unraveled the activation pathway of bis(pinacolato)diboron (B2Pin2), determining whether base-mediated cleavage occurs independently or requires direct coordination to the palladium center. The mechanism reveals a surprising concerted fusion of the oxidative addition and transmetalation steps, paving the way not only for a new avenue in the Miyaura reaction, but potentially for other cross-coupling processes. This synergy may prompt a re-evaluation of previous studies, suggesting that similar pathways could enable milder reaction conditions.
item.dc:creator: Massimo Christian D’Alterio, Eugenio Romano, Leandros P. Zorba, Gianluca Carì, Giovanni Talarico, Steven P. Nolan, Albert Poater
item.dc:title: Decoding Transmetalation in Miyaura Borylation: The Hidden Chemistry of Bis(pinacolato) Diboron and Base Effects

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Selective Hydrogenation of Nitriles to Secondary Imines Using a Pincer Manganese Complex
item.dc:description:
An earth‐abundant manganese‐based pincer complexcatalyzed the selective hydrogenation of nitriles to secondary imines. The reaction proceeds in the presence of catalytic base under H2 pressure generating NH3 as byproduct.
An earth‐abundant manganese‐based pincer phosphine complex (Ph‐PNP)Mn(Br)(CO)2, (Ph‐PNP = bis(2‐(diphenylphosphaneyl)ethyl)amine) Mn‐1 catalyzed selective hydrogenation of nitriles to secondary imines is reported. The reaction proceeds in the presence of catalytic base under H2 pressure generating NH3 as a byproduct. An optimized loading of 2 mol% Mn‐1, 20 mol% KOtBu revealed hydrogenation of benzonitrile to corresponding N‐benzylidenenbenzylamine in 92% yield after 24 h at 140°C in toluene under 40 bar H2. A wide range of substituted benzonitriles could be hydrogenated to corresponding secondary imines in good to excellent yields under the reaction conditions. The substrate scope displayed promising outcome for heteroaromatic nitriles but poor yields for aliphatic nitrile hydrogenation. Notably, the reaction does not lead to further hydrogenation of intermediate imines to form secondary amines. Stoichiometric evidences and computational studies indicate possible mechanism involving metal–ligand cooperative H2 activation and subsequent transfer of H2 to unsaturated nitrile moiety.
item.content:encoded:
An earth-abundant manganese-based pincer complexcatalyzed the selective hydrogenation of nitriles to secondary imines. The reaction proceeds in the presence of catalytic base under H2 pressure generating NH3 as byproduct.


An earth-abundant manganese-based pincer phosphine complex (Ph-PNP)Mn(Br)(CO)2, (Ph-PNP = bis(2-(diphenylphosphaneyl)ethyl)amine) Mn-1 catalyzed selective hydrogenation of nitriles to secondary imines is reported. The reaction proceeds in the presence of catalytic base under H2 pressure generating NH3 as a byproduct. An optimized loading of 2 mol% Mn-1, 20 mol% KO t Bu revealed hydrogenation of benzonitrile to corresponding N-benzylidenenbenzylamine in 92% yield after 24 h at 140°C in toluene under 40 bar H2. A wide range of substituted benzonitriles could be hydrogenated to corresponding secondary imines in good to excellent yields under the reaction conditions. The substrate scope displayed promising outcome for heteroaromatic nitriles but poor yields for aliphatic nitrile hydrogenation. Notably, the reaction does not lead to further hydrogenation of intermediate imines to form secondary amines. Stoichiometric evidences and computational studies indicate possible mechanism involving metal–ligand cooperative H2 activation and subsequent transfer of H2 to unsaturated nitrile moiety.

item.dc:title: Selective Hydrogenation of Nitriles to Secondary Imines Using a Pincer Manganese Complex

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Engineering Adjacent Cu Clusters to Modulate Single‐Fe‐Atom Sites for Enhanced CO2‐to‐CO Electrocatalytic Conversion
item.dc:description:
Based on a negatively charged polyelectrolyte nanosphere (n‐PNS) template strategy, bimetallic Cu–Fe sites anchored on hierarchical porous nitrogen‐doped carbon were constructed for enhanced CO2‐to‐CO electrocatalytic conversion.
Fine‐tuning the coordination environment and electronic structure of metal sites in metal–nitrogen–carbon (M–N–C) catalysts is an effective strategy for boosting the activity of electrocatalytic CO2 reduction reaction (CO2RR), but remains challenges. Here, a negatively charged polyelectrolyte nanosphere (n‐PNS)‐confinement‐pyrolysis strategy is developed to rationally engineer bimetallic atomic sites anchored on a hierarchical porous carbon (HPC) derived from zeolitic imidazolate framework‐8 (ZIF‐8). Through precise design of Cu species (single atoms, atomic clusters, and nanoparticles), the electronic and coordination structures of Fe single‐atom sites are effectively tailored. Notably, X‐ray photoelectron spectroscopy and X‐ray absorption spectroscopy analysis confirms that the incorporation of Cu atomic clusters (CuC) adjacent to isolated Fe single atoms (FeA) induces significant electron transfer from Fe to Cu through metal–support interactions. By virtue of this electronic modulation, the CuCFeA/HPC catalyst exhibits exceptional electrocatalytic CO2RR performance, achieving a CO Faradaic efficiency of 91% and a CO current density of 5.57 mA cm−2, as well as remarkable durability at −0.5 V versus RHE, surpassing the CuC/HPC and FeA/HPC references. This study proposes a novel approach for designing bimetallic M–N–C catalysts to achieve efficient CO2RR by utilizing the synergistic interactions between single atom and atomic cluster metal species.
item.content:encoded:
Based on a negatively charged polyelectrolyte nanosphere (n-PNS) template strategy, bimetallic Cu–Fe sites anchored on hierarchical porous nitrogen-doped carbon were constructed for enhanced CO2-to-CO electrocatalytic conversion.


Fine-tuning the coordination environment and electronic structure of metal sites in metal–nitrogen–carbon (M–N–C) catalysts is an effective strategy for boosting the activity of electrocatalytic CO2 reduction reaction (CO2RR), but remains challenges. Here, a negatively charged polyelectrolyte nanosphere (n-PNS)-confinement-pyrolysis strategy is developed to rationally engineer bimetallic atomic sites anchored on a hierarchical porous carbon (HPC) derived from zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8). Through precise design of Cu species (single atoms, atomic clusters, and nanoparticles), the electronic and coordination structures of Fe single-atom sites are effectively tailored. Notably, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and X-ray absorption spectroscopy analysis confirms that the incorporation of Cu atomic clusters (CuC) adjacent to isolated Fe single atoms (FeA) induces significant electron transfer from Fe to Cu through metal–support interactions. By virtue of this electronic modulation, the CuCFeA/HPC catalyst exhibits exceptional electrocatalytic CO2RR performance, achieving a CO Faradaic efficiency of 91% and a CO current density of 5.57 mA cm−2, as well as remarkable durability at −0.5 V versus RHE, surpassing the CuC/HPC and FeA/HPC references. This study proposes a novel approach for designing bimetallic M–N–C catalysts to achieve efficient CO2RR by utilizing the synergistic interactions between single atom and atomic cluster metal species.
item.dc:creator: Qian Luo, Wenxin Xia, Zhinan Fu, Dian Guo, Lihui Zhou, Sheng Dai
item.dc:title: Engineering Adjacent Cu Clusters to Modulate Single‐Fe‐Atom Sites for Enhanced CO2‐to‐CO Electrocatalytic Conversion

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Access to Substituted Furans via I2‐Catalyzed Oxidative Cyclization of Unsaturated Ketones
item.dc:description:
It is described a novel approach to substituted furans via iodine‐catalyzed oxidative cyclization of enones, enabling the de novo synthesis of fused polycyclic furans with potential optoelectronic applications and bioactive diaryl‐substituted furan‐3‐carboxylates.
Owing to their inherently electron‐rich nature, furan rings are susceptible to oxidation, resulting in the relative scarcity of reported methodologies for furan synthesis that depend on oxidative cyclization. Significantly, there are no documented protocols for constructing furan rings via the metal‐free oxidative cyclization of unactivated enones. Herein, we report a novel approach to substituted furans via iodine‐catalyzed oxidative cyclization of enones lacking electron‐withdrawing groups adjacent to the unsaturated ketone moiety, providing access to fused polycyclic furans for optoelectronic applications and bioactive diaryl‐substituted furan‐3‐carboxylates. This protocol differs from conventional furan synthesis strategies and opens up avenues for developing synthetic methodologies for furans from reductive equivalents of 1,4‐diketones.
item.content:encoded:
It is described a novel approach to substituted furans via iodine-catalyzed oxidative cyclization of enones, enabling the de novo synthesis of fused polycyclic furans with potential optoelectronic applications and bioactive diaryl-substituted furan-3-carboxylates.


Owing to their inherently electron-rich nature, furan rings are susceptible to oxidation, resulting in the relative scarcity of reported methodologies for furan synthesis that depend on oxidative cyclization. Significantly, there are no documented protocols for constructing furan rings via the metal-free oxidative cyclization of unactivated enones. Herein, we report a novel approach to substituted furans via iodine-catalyzed oxidative cyclization of enones lacking electron-withdrawing groups adjacent to the unsaturated ketone moiety, providing access to fused polycyclic furans for optoelectronic applications and bioactive diaryl-substituted furan-3-carboxylates. This protocol differs from conventional furan synthesis strategies and opens up avenues for developing synthetic methodologies for furans from reductive equivalents of 1,4-diketones.
item.dc:creator: Yi‐Jian Zhao, Xia You, Zhong‐Kun Yang, Xin Chen, Bing Pan, Guo‐Yu Zhou, Qun‐Li Luo, Li‐Gang Bai
item.dc:title: Access to Substituted Furans via I2‐Catalyzed Oxidative Cyclization of Unsaturated Ketones

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Domino Ring‐Opening Cyclization of Aziridines with Isatogens: A Stereoselective Synthetic Route to Fluorescent Tetrahydrooxadiazino[2,3‐a]indolones
item.dc:description:
A Cu(I)‐catalysed domino ring‐opening cyclisation (DROC) enables rapid assembly of fluorescent tetrahydrooxadiazino[2,3‐a]indolones from activated aziridines and isatogens with excellent regio‐, diastereo‐, and enantioselectivity. A one‐pot nitroalkyne cycloisomerisation/DROC sequence highlights the versatility, while optoelectronic properties reveal strong emission, solvatochromism, and high quantum efficiency.
A highly regio‐ and stereoselective Cu(I)‐catalysed domino ring‐opening cyclisation (DROC) of activated aziridines with isatogens has been developed, providing access to tricyclic fluorescent tetrahydrooxadiazino[2,3‐a]indolones bearing a substituted quaternary carbon center. The reaction proceeds under mild conditions, delivering products in up to 99% yield with excellent diastereo‐ (up to 99% dr) and enantioselectivity (99% ee for non‐racemic aziridines). A representative one‐pot sequence merging nitroalkyne cycloisomerisation with DROC of aziridines further demonstrates the versatility of this methodology. The resulting heterocycles display promising photophysical properties, including UV–visible absorption, fluorescence emission, and lifetime measurements, as well as pronounced solvatochromism. Cyclic voltammetry was employed to estimate electrochemical band gaps and HOMO–LUMO energy levels, and the compounds exhibit high fluorescence efficiency (quantum yields up to 0.6), highlighting their potential for opto‐electronic applications.
item.content:encoded:
A Cu(I)-catalysed domino ring-opening cyclisation (DROC) enables rapid assembly of fluorescent tetrahydrooxadiazino[2,3-a]indolones from activated aziridines and isatogens with excellent regio-, diastereo-, and enantioselectivity. A one-pot nitroalkyne cycloisomerisation/DROC sequence highlights the versatility, while optoelectronic properties reveal strong emission, solvatochromism, and high quantum efficiency.


A highly regio- and stereoselective Cu(I)-catalysed domino ring-opening cyclisation (DROC) of activated aziridines with isatogens has been developed, providing access to tricyclic fluorescent tetrahydrooxadiazino[2,3-a]indolones bearing a substituted quaternary carbon center. The reaction proceeds under mild conditions, delivering products in up to 99% yield with excellent diastereo- (up to 99% dr) and enantioselectivity (99% ee for non-racemic aziridines). A representative one-pot sequence merging nitroalkyne cycloisomerisation with DROC of aziridines further demonstrates the versatility of this methodology. The resulting heterocycles display promising photophysical properties, including UV–visible absorption, fluorescence emission, and lifetime measurements, as well as pronounced solvatochromism. Cyclic voltammetry was employed to estimate electrochemical band gaps and HOMO–LUMO energy levels, and the compounds exhibit high fluorescence efficiency (quantum yields up to 0.6), highlighting their potential for opto-electronic applications.

item.dc:title: Domino Ring‐Opening Cyclization of Aziridines with Isatogens: A Stereoselective Synthetic Route to Fluorescent Tetrahydrooxadiazino[2,3‐a]indolones

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: N‐Amidinyl‐Benzodiazaborines as Novel Boron‐Based Scaffolds: Synthesis and Application in Retromer Stabilization
item.dc:description:
In this work, we successfully synthesized a library of novel N‐amidinyl‐benzodiazaborines, showing how these heteroaryl guanyl hydrazones are able to stabilize the retromer, a protein complex involved in the disposal of misfolded and aggregated proteins, whose malfunction is observed in multiple neurodegenerative diseases.
Diazaborines, a class of boron‐containing compounds known for their antibacterial activity, are synthesized from 2‐formylphenylboronic acid and hydrazines. Yet, the use of aminoguanidine has never been explored; thus, no examples of N‐amidinyl‐benzodiazaborines have been reported to date. Herein, we computationally and experimentally investigated the synthesis and characterization of novel N‐amidinyl‐benzodiazaborines, showing how these heteroaryl guanyl hydrazones are able to stabilize the retromer, a protein complex whose malfunction impairs the disposal of neurotoxic protein aggregates in neurodegenerative diseases such as Parkinson's disease (PD) and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS).
item.content:encoded:
In this work, we successfully synthesized a library of novel N-amidinyl-benzodiazaborines, showing how these heteroaryl guanyl hydrazones are able to stabilize the retromer, a protein complex involved in the disposal of misfolded and aggregated proteins, whose malfunction is observed in multiple neurodegenerative diseases.


Diazaborines, a class of boron-containing compounds known for their antibacterial activity, are synthesized from 2-formylphenylboronic acid and hydrazines. Yet, the use of aminoguanidine has never been explored; thus, no examples of N-amidinyl-benzodiazaborines have been reported to date. Herein, we computationally and experimentally investigated the synthesis and characterization of novel N-amidinyl-benzodiazaborines, showing how these heteroaryl guanyl hydrazones are able to stabilize the retromer, a protein complex whose malfunction impairs the disposal of neurotoxic protein aggregates in neurodegenerative diseases such as Parkinson's disease (PD) and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS).
item.dc:creator: Alessandro Dimasi, Arianna Montoli, Michele Buonaguro, Leonardo Maiorana, Giovanni Macetti, Eleonora Colombo, Luca Muzio, Greta Donati, Daniele Passarella, Pierfausto Seneci, Valerio Fasano
item.dc:title: N‐Amidinyl‐Benzodiazaborines as Novel Boron‐Based Scaffolds: Synthesis and Application in Retromer Stabilization

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Ni‐Catalyzed Selective Crosscoupling for Diarylmethane‐Containing Boronic Ester Synthesis
item.dc:description:
Nickel‐catalyzed, base‐free reductive cross‐coupling of arylboronic esters and benzyl halides enables direct synthesis of diarylmethane‐containing boronic esters. DFT calculations support a kinetically favored radical pathway that governs selectivity. The method exhibits broad scope, is scalable, and is applied to the synthesis of ketoprofen and antihistamine drugs. The products further show synergistic anticancer activity with bupivacaine.
Boron‐functionalized diarylmethanes represent valuable scaffolds in medicinal chemistry, yet their efficient synthesis remains challenging due to the instability and reactivity of boronic ester motifs. Herein, we report a modular nickel‐catalyzed crosselectrophile coupling strategy for the selective construction of diarylmethane‐containing boronic esters from readily available arylboronic esters and benzyl halides. The reaction proceeds under mild, base‐free conditions, delivering a wide range of structurally diverse products in up to 94% yield with excellent chemoselectivity. Mechanistic investigations, supported by density functional theory (DFT) calculations and stoichiometric experiments, reveal a competitive pathway scenario in which a single‐electron transfer (SET)‐mediated radical pathway is kinetically favored and governs the observed selectivity. The protocol exhibits broad substrate scope and high functional group tolerance and is amenable to gram‐scale synthesis without chromatographic purification. Furthermore, the synthetic utility is demonstrated through streamlined access to pharmaceutically relevant molecules, including ketoprofen and antihistamine derivatives. Notably, the resulting boronic ester products display bioactivity‐modulating potential, showing synergistic cytotoxicity with bupivacaine in cancer cell models. This work provides a practical and mechanistically informed platform for the synthesis of functionalized diarylmethanes and expands the utility of boron‐containing building blocks in drug discovery and development.
item.content:encoded:
Nickel-catalyzed, base-free reductive cross-coupling of arylboronic esters and benzyl halides enables direct synthesis of diarylmethane-containing boronic esters. DFT calculations support a kinetically favored radical pathway that governs selectivity. The method exhibits broad scope, is scalable, and is applied to the synthesis of ketoprofen and antihistamine drugs. The products further show synergistic anticancer activity with bupivacaine.


Boron-functionalized diarylmethanes represent valuable scaffolds in medicinal chemistry, yet their efficient synthesis remains challenging due to the instability and reactivity of boronic ester motifs. Herein, we report a modular nickel-catalyzed crosselectrophile coupling strategy for the selective construction of diarylmethane-containing boronic esters from readily available arylboronic esters and benzyl halides. The reaction proceeds under mild, base-free conditions, delivering a wide range of structurally diverse products in up to 94% yield with excellent chemoselectivity. Mechanistic investigations, supported by density functional theory (DFT) calculations and stoichiometric experiments, reveal a competitive pathway scenario in which a single-electron transfer (SET)-mediated radical pathway is kinetically favored and governs the observed selectivity. The protocol exhibits broad substrate scope and high functional group tolerance and is amenable to gram-scale synthesis without chromatographic purification. Furthermore, the synthetic utility is demonstrated through streamlined access to pharmaceutically relevant molecules, including ketoprofen and antihistamine derivatives. Notably, the resulting boronic ester products display bioactivity-modulating potential, showing synergistic cytotoxicity with bupivacaine in cancer cell models. This work provides a practical and mechanistically informed platform for the synthesis of functionalized diarylmethanes and expands the utility of boron-containing building blocks in drug discovery and development.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Enantioselective Synthesis of Lactic Acid From CO2 Using a Novel Pyruvate Decarboxylase in a Scalable Biocatalytic Cascade
item.dc:description:
We report a scalable biocatalytic cascade that converts CO2 and acetaldehyde to pyruvate via a newly characterized pyruvate decarboxylase, followed by stereoselective reduction to lactic acid. The system enables fully switchable enantioselectivity using either L‐ or D‐lactate dehydrogenases.
Biocatalytic cascades offer a promising route for CO2‐fixation into valuable chemicals, addressing the urgent need for efficient, sustainable technologies to reduce CO2 emissions. This paper describes an enzymatic route converting gaseous CO2 and acetaldehyde into enantiopure lactic acid, widely used in diverse industries. A newly characterized pyruvate decarboxylase from Neoasia chiangmaiensis (NcPDC) enabled acetaldehyde carboxylation to pyruvate. To suppress the competing carboligation to acetoin, acetaldehyde was reversibly trapped with Tris. Pyruvate was reduced to lactate by lactate dehydrogenase, coupled with glucose dehydrogenase for NADH regeneration via D‐glucose oxidation to D‐gluconic acid. Up to 65% lactate yield was achieved. Repeated acetaldehyde dosing resulted in a 27 mM titer, representing a >100‐fold improvement over previous reports. At 0.5 L scale, using a gas mixture mimicking industrial‐grade CO2, we obtained 21 mM D‐(–)‐lactic acid, 42% yield and >98% e.e., demonstrating scalability and robustness. Finally, replacing the D‐(–)‐selective lactate dehydrogenase with an L‐(+)‐selective variant at small scale enabled production of L‐(+)‐lactic acid at 41% yield and >93% e.e, allowing switchable access to either enantiomer. A volumetric productivity of 1.1 × 10−2 g L−1 h−1 ranks among the most efficient minimal enzymatic routes developed to date for CO2‐to‐lactate conversion.
item.content:encoded:
We report a scalable biocatalytic cascade that converts CO2 and acetaldehyde to pyruvate via a newly characterized pyruvate decarboxylase, followed by stereoselective reduction to lactic acid. The system enables fully switchable enantioselectivity using either L- or D-lactate dehydrogenases.


Biocatalytic cascades offer a promising route for CO2-fixation into valuable chemicals, addressing the urgent need for efficient, sustainable technologies to reduce CO2 emissions. This paper describes an enzymatic route converting gaseous CO2 and acetaldehyde into enantiopure lactic acid, widely used in diverse industries. A newly characterized pyruvate decarboxylase from Neoasia chiangmaiensis (NcPDC) enabled acetaldehyde carboxylation to pyruvate. To suppress the competing carboligation to acetoin, acetaldehyde was reversibly trapped with Tris. Pyruvate was reduced to lactate by lactate dehydrogenase, coupled with glucose dehydrogenase for NADH regeneration via D-glucose oxidation to D-gluconic acid. Up to 65% lactate yield was achieved. Repeated acetaldehyde dosing resulted in a 27 mM titer, representing a >100-fold improvement over previous reports. At 0.5 L scale, using a gas mixture mimicking industrial-grade CO2, we obtained 21 mM D-(–)-lactic acid, 42% yield and >98% e.e., demonstrating scalability and robustness. Finally, replacing the D-(–)-selective lactate dehydrogenase with an L-(+)-selective variant at small scale enabled production of L-(+)-lactic acid at 41% yield and >93% e.e, allowing switchable access to either enantiomer. A volumetric productivity of 1.1 × 10−2 g L−1 h−1 ranks among the most efficient minimal enzymatic routes developed to date for CO2-to-lactate conversion.
item.dc:creator: Silvia Fruncillo, Daan M. van Vliet, Jurgen J. Mateman, Dirk C. van Seijst, Jan Stoutjesdijk, Jason van Rooyen, Akash Pandya, James Galman, Annette Alcasabas, Tom A. Ewing
item.dc:title: Enantioselective Synthesis of Lactic Acid From CO2 Using a Novel Pyruvate Decarboxylase in a Scalable Biocatalytic Cascade

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Efficient Access to Polycyclic Scaffolds via a Dearomatization/Polyene Cyclization Cascade
item.dc:description:
A novel activation mode for polyene cyclization is established by synergistically integrating phenol dearomatization. This hypervalent iodine‐mediated cascade enables the one‐step assembly of complex polycyclic skeletons from simple phenolic precursors, providing efficient access to motifs such as spirocyclohexadienones and trans‐benzo[f]isoquinoline frameworks with high yield and stereoselectivity.
Herein, we report a hypervalent iodine‐mediated tandem reaction that synergistically integrates phenol dearomatization with polyene cyclization for the efficient construction of complex polycyclic frameworks. This method enables the one‐step stereoselective synthesis of trans‐benzo[f]isoquinoline and spirocyclohexadienone skeletons. The reaction proceeds rapidly under mild conditions, delivering products in high yields with excellent diastereoselectivity. This work not only expands the application of hypervalent iodine reagents in organic synthesis but also provides a novel and efficient strategy for accessing polycyclic natural product scaffolds.
item.content:encoded:
A novel activation mode for polyene cyclization is established by synergistically integrating phenol dearomatization. This hypervalent iodine-mediated cascade enables the one-step assembly of complex polycyclic skeletons from simple phenolic precursors, providing efficient access to motifs such as spirocyclohexadienones and trans-benzo[f]isoquinoline frameworks with high yield and stereoselectivity.


Herein, we report a hypervalent iodine-mediated tandem reaction that synergistically integrates phenol dearomatization with polyene cyclization for the efficient construction of complex polycyclic frameworks. This method enables the one-step stereoselective synthesis of trans-benzo[f]isoquinoline and spirocyclohexadienone skeletons. The reaction proceeds rapidly under mild conditions, delivering products in high yields with excellent diastereoselectivity. This work not only expands the application of hypervalent iodine reagents in organic synthesis but also provides a novel and efficient strategy for accessing polycyclic natural product scaffolds.
item.dc:creator: Sihan Zhang, Runze Li, Bingsheng Wang, Ye Zhang, Daoyong Zhu, Wen Bao, Shao‐Hua Wang
item.dc:title: Efficient Access to Polycyclic Scaffolds via a Dearomatization/Polyene Cyclization Cascade

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Photocatalytic Radical Cascade Cyclization of Unsaturated Aldehyde Hydrazones: Access to Difluoroalkylated 1,2‐Diazepines
item.dc:description:
The visible‐light photocatalyzed radical cascade cyclization of unsaturated aldehyde hydrazones for the synthesis of difluoroalkylated 1,2‐diazepines was developed.
1,2‐Diazepines as a representative seven‐membered ring azaheterocycles are widely present in natural products and bioactive compounds. The efficient method to construct 1,2‐diazepine motif is still a challenge and is rare. Here, a visible‐light‐catalytic radical cascade cyclization of unsaturated aldehyde hydrazones for the synthesis of difluoroalkylated 1,2‐diazepines was developed for the first time. The reaction was carried out by using Eosin Y as an organic photocatalyst and alkyl difluoroacetates as the key difluoroalkylation reagent. Under mild and metal‐free conditions, various difluoroalkylated 1,2‐diazacyclononanes were efficiently synthesized. The present catalytic protocol paved the way to obtain functionalized 1,2‐diazepines.
item.content:encoded:
The visible-light photocatalyzed radical cascade cyclization of unsaturated aldehyde hydrazones for the synthesis of difluoroalkylated 1,2-diazepines was developed.


1,2-Diazepines as a representative seven-membered ring azaheterocycles are widely present in natural products and bioactive compounds. The efficient method to construct 1,2-diazepine motif is still a challenge and is rare. Here, a visible-light-catalytic radical cascade cyclization of unsaturated aldehyde hydrazones for the synthesis of difluoroalkylated 1,2-diazepines was developed for the first time. The reaction was carried out by using Eosin Y as an organic photocatalyst and alkyl difluoroacetates as the key difluoroalkylation reagent. Under mild and metal-free conditions, various difluoroalkylated 1,2-diazacyclononanes were efficiently synthesized. The present catalytic protocol paved the way to obtain functionalized 1,2-diazepines.

item.dc:title: Photocatalytic Radical Cascade Cyclization of Unsaturated Aldehyde Hydrazones: Access to Difluoroalkylated 1,2‐Diazepines

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Recent Advances in Catalytic [5 + 3] Cycloadditions
item.dc:description:
The current review summarizes the various catalytic [5 + 3] cycloadditions in the development of structurally diverse eight‐membered cyclic architectures.
Higher‐order cycloadditions (HOCs) offer a robust and convergent strategy for the rapid assembly of complex molecular architectures. These transformations enable direct access to ring systems that are otherwise challenging to construct. Among them, the [5 + 3] cycloaddition has emerged as a versatile platform for the synthesis of eight‐membered carbo‐ and heterocycles, which are widely represented in bioactive natural products, pharmaceuticals, and functional materials. This review provides a comprehensive overview of developments in catalytic [5 + 3] cycloadditions from 2003 to the present, encompassing both transition metals and organocatalytic systems. Emphasis is placed on catalyst class, ligand design, activation mode, and substrate scope, as well as their influence on chemo‐, regio‐, and stereoselectivity, together with comparative performance data. Representative mechanistic proposals reported in the literature are discussed where relevant. Although most catalytic [5 + 3] processes proceed through stepwise pathways and are therefore best described as formal [5 + 3] cycloadditions, true pericyclic [5π + 3π] cycloadditions are comparatively rare. Where applicable, this mechanistic distinction is clearly indicated in the corresponding sections.
item.content:encoded:
The current review summarizes the various catalytic [5 + 3] cycloadditions in the development of structurally diverse eight-membered cyclic architectures.


Higher-order cycloadditions (HOCs) offer a robust and convergent strategy for the rapid assembly of complex molecular architectures. These transformations enable direct access to ring systems that are otherwise challenging to construct. Among them, the [5 + 3] cycloaddition has emerged as a versatile platform for the synthesis of eight-membered carbo- and heterocycles, which are widely represented in bioactive natural products, pharmaceuticals, and functional materials. This review provides a comprehensive overview of developments in catalytic [5 + 3] cycloadditions from 2003 to the present, encompassing both transition metals and organocatalytic systems. Emphasis is placed on catalyst class, ligand design, activation mode, and substrate scope, as well as their influence on chemo-, regio-, and stereoselectivity, together with comparative performance data. Representative mechanistic proposals reported in the literature are discussed where relevant. Although most catalytic [5 + 3] processes proceed through stepwise pathways and are therefore best described as formal [5 + 3] cycloadditions, true pericyclic [5π + 3π] cycloadditions are comparatively rare. Where applicable, this mechanistic distinction is clearly indicated in the corresponding sections.
item.dc:creator: Smile, Vipan Kumar, Rupesh Kumar, Dinesh Mahajan, Gaurav Bhargava
item.dc:title: Recent Advances in Catalytic [5 + 3] Cycloadditions

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Photo‐/Electrochemical Site‐Selective Radical α‐C(sp3)–H Functionalization of Amines: Four Green Strategies for Applications, Challenges, and Opportunities
item.dc:description:
This review highlights recent advances in photo‐ or electrochemical site‐selective radical N‐C(sp3)–H functionalization, emphasizing on four green strategies and emerging applications in amines.
The activation of N‐C(sp3)H bonds is essential in various fields, such as bioactive compounds, natural products, pharmaceuticals, and materials science, yet traditional protocols for the radical α‐C(sp3)–H functionalization of amines require harsh conditions, suffer from low atom economy, and rely on stoichiometric hazardous oxidants such as peroxides. Hence, the direct activation of the N‐C(sp3)H bond under mild or transition‐metal‐free conditions remains a significant challenge. Recently, emerging strategies such as photo‐ or electroinduced single‐electron oxidation (SEO), hydrogen atom transfer (HAT), electron donor–acceptor (EDA) complex formation, and ligand‐to‐metal charge transfer (LMCT) have enabled transformations under mild conditions. Despite substantial progress and growing interest in this area, reviews on photo‐/electrochemical C(sp3)–H bond activation and functionalization remain scarce. This article summarizes recent advances (2016–2025) in this rapidly expanding research area, aiming to provide guidance for the sustainable application of photo‐/electrochemical radical α‐C(sp3)–H functionalization reactions and to stimulate further progress in this field.
item.content:encoded:
This review highlights recent advances in photo- or electrochemical site-selective radical N-C(sp3)–H functionalization, emphasizing on four green strategies and emerging applications in amines.


The activation of N-C(sp3)H bonds is essential in various fields, such as bioactive compounds, natural products, pharmaceuticals, and materials science, yet traditional protocols for the radical α-C(sp3)–H functionalization of amines require harsh conditions, suffer from low atom economy, and rely on stoichiometric hazardous oxidants such as peroxides. Hence, the direct activation of the N-C(sp3)H bond under mild or transition-metal-free conditions remains a significant challenge. Recently, emerging strategies such as photo- or electroinduced single-electron oxidation (SEO), hydrogen atom transfer (HAT), electron donor–acceptor (EDA) complex formation, and ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) have enabled transformations under mild conditions. Despite substantial progress and growing interest in this area, reviews on photo-/electrochemical C(sp3)–H bond activation and functionalization remain scarce. This article summarizes recent advances (2016–2025) in this rapidly expanding research area, aiming to provide guidance for the sustainable application of photo-/electrochemical radical α-C(sp3)–H functionalization reactions and to stimulate further progress in this field.

item.dc:title: Photo‐/Electrochemical Site‐Selective Radical α‐C(sp3)–H Functionalization of Amines: Four Green Strategies for Applications, Challenges, and Opportunities

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Recent Developments in Defluorinative Functionalization of β‐Trifluoromethyl Enones
item.dc:description: The functionalization of perfluoroalkylated or fluorinated compounds via selective CF bond activation represents an emerging method for the synthesis of structurally diverse and complex molecules. However, due to the inherent low reactivity of CF bonds in terms of kinetic and thermodynamic stabilities, coupled with the steric hindrance caused by the shielding effect of the three fluorine atoms, methods that transform C(sp3)F bonds within the trifluoromethyl group are challenging but highly desirable. β‐Trifluoromethyl enones (β‐CF3‐enones), a class of versatile building blocks, include three reactive sites within their structure: an electrophilic carbonyl moiety, an α,β‐unsaturated alkenyl system, and a β‐attached CF3 group. Over the past decades, the defluorinative transformation of β‐trifluoromethyl enones, involving one to three CF bond cleavages, coupled with the simultaneous functionalization of the α,β‐unsaturated alkenyl system and/or the carbonyl group, unlocks their new versatility in synthetic chemistry. In this review, we categorize these recent developments into four classes, including (1) hydrodefluorination of β‐trifluoromethyl enones under reductive conditions, (2) hydrodefluorophosphorylation and hydrodefluoroamination of β‐trifluoromethyl enones in the presence of a reductant and P‐/N‐nucleophiles, (3) defluorophosphination and/or defluorophosphorylation of β‐trifluoromethyl enones with P‐nucleophiles, and (4) defluorinative cyclization of β‐trifluoromethyl enones for the construction of diverse heterocycles. Specific emphasis is placed on the discussion of the reaction design, reaction mechanisms, and the substrate scopes. Further perspective on the future development of this growing area is also provided.
item.content:encoded: The functionalization of perfluoroalkylated or fluorinated compounds via selective CF bond activation represents an emerging method for the synthesis of structurally diverse and complex molecules. However, due to the inherent low reactivity of CF bonds in terms of kinetic and thermodynamic stabilities, coupled with the steric hindrance caused by the shielding effect of the three fluorine atoms, methods that transform C(sp3)F bonds within the trifluoromethyl group are challenging but highly desirable. β-Trifluoromethyl enones (β-CF3-enones), a class of versatile building blocks, include three reactive sites within their structure: an electrophilic carbonyl moiety, an α,β-unsaturated alkenyl system, and a β-attached CF3 group. Over the past decades, the defluorinative transformation of β-trifluoromethyl enones, involving one to three CF bond cleavages, coupled with the simultaneous functionalization of the α,β-unsaturated alkenyl system and/or the carbonyl group, unlocks their new versatility in synthetic chemistry. In this review, we categorize these recent developments into four classes, including (1) hydrodefluorination of β-trifluoromethyl enones under reductive conditions, (2) hydrodefluorophosphorylation and hydrodefluoroamination of β-trifluoromethyl enones in the presence of a reductant and P-/N-nucleophiles, (3) defluorophosphination and/or defluorophosphorylation of β-trifluoromethyl enones with P-nucleophiles, and (4) defluorinative cyclization of β-trifluoromethyl enones for the construction of diverse heterocycles. Specific emphasis is placed on the discussion of the reaction design, reaction mechanisms, and the substrate scopes. Further perspective on the future development of this growing area is also provided.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Recent Progresses in On‐Surface Glaser Coupling
item.dc:description:
On‐surface Glaser coupling transforms terminal alkynes into atomically precise diyne‐linked nanoarchitectures. Substrate templating, chemical group tuning, steric shielding, and external stimuli (light, O2, and Cu+) steer the reactivity of alkynes toward highly selective Glaser coupling.
On‐surface synthesis offers robust and versatile routes for fabricating atomically precise carbon‐rich nanomaterials through the transformation of covalent coupling reactions from solution to two‐dimensional confined interfaces. The dehydrogenative homocoupling (Glaser coupling) of terminal alkynes and their derivatives provide a direct and efficient pathway to form 1,3‐diyne linkages and molecular hydrogen as the sole byproduct. The linear geometry and high directionality of alkynes enable the bottom‐up construction of extended low‐dimensional carbon frameworks with high structural regularity. The process exhibits broad substrate compatibility from catalytically active coinage metal surfaces to electronically inert insulating surfaces. The interactions between precursor and surface template dictate product selectivity, orientation, and long‐range order. Nevertheless, competing pathways such as enyne formation, cyclotrimerization, and radical rearrangements exist along with the Glaser coupling. And the surface‐dependent reactivity, chemical group functionality influence, steric effect, and external stimuli (e.g., light, H2O or O2 coadsorption, and Cu2+ additives) can steer the selectivity toward Glaser coupling. Recent mechanistic insights by SPM and DFT simulations reveal transient intermediates, hydrogen‐transfer steps, and Glaser‐type dimers, clarifying the coupling pathway. This article comprehensively discusses the breakthroughs in the formation of atomically precise diyne‐linked nanostructures via Glaser coupling of alkynes.
item.content:encoded:
On-surface Glaser coupling transforms terminal alkynes into atomically precise diyne-linked nanoarchitectures. Substrate templating, chemical group tuning, steric shielding, and external stimuli (light, O2, and Cu+) steer the reactivity of alkynes toward highly selective Glaser coupling.


On-surface synthesis offers robust and versatile routes for fabricating atomically precise carbon-rich nanomaterials through the transformation of covalent coupling reactions from solution to two-dimensional confined interfaces. The linear geometry and high directionality of alkynes enable the bottom-up construction of extended low-dimensional carbon frameworks with high structural regularity. The process exhibits broad substrate compatibility from catalytically active coinage metal surfaces to electronically inert insulating surfaces. The interactions between precursor and surface template dictate product selectivity, orientation, and long-range order. Nevertheless, competing pathways such as enyne formation, cyclotrimerization, and radical rearrangements exist along with the Glaser coupling. And the surface-dependent reactivity, chemical group functionality influence, steric effect, and external stimuli (e.g., light, H2O or O2 coadsorption, and Cu2+ additives) can steer the selectivity toward Glaser coupling. Recent mechanistic insights by SPM and DFT simulations reveal transient intermediates, hydrogen-transfer steps, and Glaser-type dimers, clarifying the coupling pathway. This article comprehensively discusses the breakthroughs in the formation of atomically precise diyne-linked nanostructures via Glaser coupling of alkynes.
